quote: Systems like DALL-E 2, GPT-3, Flamingo, and Gato appear to be mind-blowing, but nobody who has looked at them carefully would confuse them for human intelligence.
claim: Current large AI systems like DALL-E 2, GPT-3, Flamingo, and Gato do not exhibit anything resembling human intelligence when examined carefully.

quote: So let's be honest: scaling hasn't worked yet.
claim: Scaling up neural networks has not yet produced systems that reliably understand the world.

quote: the abiding problem of discomprehension might—despite the immense progress—signal the need for a fundamental rethink
claim: Persistent comprehension failures in scaled-up AI systems may indicate the need for a fundamental rethink of the approach.

quote: His statement that "[neural] nets have no issue creating [symbols] and manipulating them" misses both history and reality.
claim: Neural networks have not demonstrated the ability to reliably create and manipulate symbols, contrary to claims by scaling advocates.

quote: symbolic descriptions like "red cube on blue cube" still elude the state-of-the-art in 2022
claim: State-of-the-art AI systems in 2022 still cannot reliably interpret symbolic spatial descriptions like 'red cube on blue cube'.

quote: Virtually all of the world's software, from web browsers to spreadsheets to word processors, still depends on knowledge engineering, and Sutton sells that short.
claim: Most of the world's functional software still relies on knowledge engineering rather than big data approaches.

quote: no current AI is remotely close to being able to read an arbitrary text with enough comprehension to be able to build a model of what a speaker is saying and intends to accomplish
claim: No current AI system can read arbitrary text and build a model of what the speaker is saying and intending.

quote: Success on a few problems with a particular strategy in no way guarantees that we can solve all problems in a similar way.
claim: Success of deep learning on specific problems does not guarantee it can solve all AI problems.

quote: the failure modes (unreliability, bizarre errors, failures in compositionality and discomprehension) have not changed since Fodor and Pinker pointed them out (in separate articles) in 1988
claim: The fundamental failure modes of neural networks (unreliability, bizarre errors, compositionality failures) have persisted unchanged since they were first identified in 1988.

quote: I am fully with Murray Shanahan when he writes "I see very little in Gato to suggest that scaling alone will get us to human-level generalisation."
claim: There is very little evidence that scaling alone will achieve human-level generalization.

quote: it doesn't work like the brain, it doesn't learn like a child, it doesn't understand language, it doesn't align with human values, and it can't be trusted with mission-critical tasks
claim: Systems like Gato do not work like the brain, learn like a child, understand language, align with human values, or merit trust for mission-critical tasks.

quote: If we are to build AGI, we are going to need to learn something from humans, how they reason and understand the physical world, and how they represent and acquire language and complex concepts.
claim: Building AGI will require incorporating insights from human cognition, including how humans reason about the physical world and acquire language and concepts.

quote: errors in 41 of the 77 articles the bot wrote
claim: CNET's AI-generated articles had errors in 41 of 77 articles, demonstrating that automated content generation produces unreliable output.

quote: the closer they get to perfect, the easier it is for mere mortals to space out
claim: As AI systems improve in surface fluency, humans become worse at catching their errors due to decreased vigilance.

quote: That's going to happen a whole lot more this year, with synthetic articles. Too many people are starting to count on systems that inherently can't keep track of the truth.
claim: AI-generated misinformation in synthetic articles will increase substantially because LLMs inherently cannot track truth.

quote: The stakes may get even higher if people start to rely on immature AI—which is all we have got, so far—for legal or medical advice.
claim: All current AI is immature, and relying on it for legal or medical advice poses serious risks.

quote: large language models didn't represent robust world models of how events fold over time; I stand by this 100% even today
claim: Large language models do not represent robust world models of how events unfold over time.

quote: I still see this as the central flaw in large language models.
claim: The lack of world models that represent how things unfold over time is the central flaw in large language models.

quote: My goalposts haven't shifted; I still want A(G)I that knows what the f___ it's talking about, and GPT still doesn't.
claim: GPT systems still do not possess genuine understanding of what they are talking about.

quote: Neural networks in the early 1990s struggled with compositionality (interpreting complex, hierarchical wholes in terms of their parts), abstraction, and plain old silly errors.
claim: Neural networks have struggled with compositionality, abstraction, and silly errors since the early 1990s.

quote: pretty much the same problems remained when hidden layers (then just one, now many) were introduced
claim: Adding more hidden layers to neural networks did not solve the fundamental problems of compositionality and abstraction.

quote: an inability to keep track of knowledge of instances independently of kinds, seems to underlie the rampant hallucinations
claim: LLM hallucinations are caused by an inability to keep track of knowledge about specific instances independently of general categories.

quote: it counts as hitting a wall if you keep trying to do the same thing over and over and don't succeed, even if you succeed on other dimensions
claim: Deep learning has hit a wall on core problems like abstraction and compositionality, even as it has made progress on other dimensions.

quote: anyone who thinks we can get to AGI without solving abstraction, compositionality, and the type-token distinction is kidding themselves
claim: AGI cannot be achieved without solving abstraction, compositionality, and the type-token distinction.

quote: Our new systems work better, but they are still superficial, fine for lab demos but often at a loss in the real world. Death by a thousand edge cases is still the norm
claim: Current AI systems are superficial and fail in the real world through accumulation of edge-case failures.

quote: On the highway towards Human-Level AI, Large Language Model is an off-ramp.
claim: LLMs are not on the path to human-level AI; they are a diversion from it. Marcus explicitly endorses LeCun's statement.

quote: LLMs have a more superficial understanding of the world than a house cat.
claim: LLMs have a more superficial understanding of the world than a house cat. Marcus explicitly endorses LeCun's statement.

quote: the general public hasn't quite realized what's not possible yet
claim: The general public does not yet understand the limitations of what LLMs like ChatGPT cannot do.

trigger: Google Bard demo and Bing/GPT launch
quote: a chatbot-induced hallucination cost Alphabet $100 billion
claim: A chatbot hallucination in Google's Bard demo cost Alphabet $100 billion in market value.

trigger: Google Bard demo and Bing/GPT launch
quote: should anybody blithely accept the reassurances of some very interested parties when AI has such a long history of demos that never quite make it to solid product? I don't think so.
claim: AI has a long history of impressive demos that never become solid products, and corporate reassurances about fixing AI errors should not be accepted uncritically.

trigger: Google Bard demo and Bing/GPT launch
quote: Scaling neural network models—making them bigger—has made their faux writing more and more authoritative-sounding, but not more and more truthful.
claim: Scaling neural networks makes their output sound more authoritative but does not make it more truthful.

trigger: Google Bard demo and Bing/GPT launch
quote: Hallucinations are in their silicon blood, a byproduct of the way they compress their inputs, losing track of factual relations in the process
claim: Hallucinations are an inherent architectural byproduct of how LLMs compress inputs, causing them to lose track of factual relations.

trigger: Google Bard demo and Bing/GPT launch
quote: I do actually think that these problems will eventually get ironed out, possibly after some important fresh discoveries are made, but whether they get ironed out soon is another matter entirely.
claim: AI hallucination problems will eventually be solved, but likely require fresh discoveries and may take years or decades.

trigger: Google Bard demo and Bing/GPT launch
quote: If they don't get ironed out soon —and they might not– people might quickly tire of chat based search
claim: If hallucination problems are not solved soon, users may abandon chat-based search and return to traditional search.

trigger: Google Bard demo and Bing/GPT launch
quote: Since neither company has yet subjected their products to full scientific review, it's impossible to say which is more trustworthy; it might well turn out that Google's new product is actually more reliable.
claim: Neither Google's nor Microsoft's chat search products have been subjected to proper scientific review, making reliability comparisons impossible.

quote: Personally I'd put the probability that we need to shift at 90%
claim: There is approximately a 90% probability that AI needs a paradigm shift beyond current approaches to reach AGI.

quote: I would put the probability that we will need to embrace symbol-manipulation as part of the mix at 80%
claim: There is approximately an 80% probability that symbol manipulation will need to be part of the mix to achieve AGI.

quote: large-scale symbolic knowledge will be crucial
claim: Large-scale symbolic knowledge will be crucial for AGI, whenever it arrives.

quote: explicit cognitive models will be crucial
claim: Explicit cognitive models will be crucial for AGI.

quote: operations over variables (including storing, retrieving and comparing values) will be crucial
claim: Operations over variables (storing, retrieving, comparing values) will be crucial for AGI.

quote: an explicit type/token distinction will be crucial
claim: An explicit type/token distinction will be crucial for AGI.

quote: can we get to AGI and reliable, no-goof reasoning, handling all the challenges that LeCun and I have been discussing, with scaling and CNNs + RNNs + Transformers alone? In my view, no way; I think the actual odds are less than 20%.
claim: There is less than a 20% chance that AGI can be achieved with scaling and CNNs + RNNs + Transformers alone.

quote: we are still missing some fundamental concepts
claim: Fundamental concepts needed for human-level AI are still missing from current approaches. Marcus explicitly endorses LeCun's statement.

quote: loads of things scale, but not at all
claim: Large language models show improvement with scale on some measures but not on others, particularly reasoning and common sense.

quote: the system doesn't understand what the prompt "a horse riding an astronaut" means
claim: Text-to-image systems like Imagen do not understand the semantic meaning of their text prompts.

quote: the network does something more holistic and approximate, a bit like keyword matching and a lot less like deep language understanding
claim: Text-to-image systems like Imagen rely on approximate keyword matching rather than genuine language understanding.

quote: What one really needs, and no one yet knows how to build, is a system that can derive semantics of wholes from their parts as a function of their syntax.
claim: No one currently knows how to build an AI system that derives compositional semantics from syntax.

quote: the fact that, if you play around with prompts long enough, you can eventually get what you want, is relevant to some forms of human-in-the-loop AI but it won't cut the mustard for reliable AI
claim: Prompt engineering to coax correct outputs from AI systems is insufficient for building reliable AI.

quote: all these systems map phrases into a space of images, but the phrases themselves are represented as strings, and not, say, as hierarchical structures such as the syntactic trees one finds in linguistics
claim: Current text-to-image systems represent phrases as flat strings rather than hierarchical syntactic structures, which is why they fail at compositional understanding.

quote: systems like GPT-3 provide remarkably little in the way of explanation. They don't tell us why the world is as it is; they merely mimic statistical patterns of how language has been used in their immense databases.
claim: LLMs like GPT-3 merely mimic statistical patterns of language use and provide no explanatory value about how the world works.

quote: the mere capacity to predict sequences of words doesn't, by itself, necessarily tell us much, scientifically, about why a human might in this context produce any given word
claim: The ability to predict word sequences does not reveal anything about human language cognition.

quote: systems like GPT-3 come about as close to blank slates as is feasible
claim: LLMs like GPT-3 approximate blank-slate learning systems, relying on massive data rather than prior knowledge.

quote: the accuracy of models like GPT-3 depends highly on the resemblance of specific tests items to the details of what does or does not appear in the database of training examples
claim: LLM accuracy on tasks like arithmetic depends heavily on whether specific test items resemble training examples, rather than reflecting abstract understanding.

quote: however good their impression of the statistics of human text might be, their comprehension of the world is scattershot, bound to the particulars of what input they are trained on, and lacking in genuine abstraction
claim: LLMs lack genuine abstraction and their comprehension is bound to the particulars of their training data.

quote: They are an embarrassment to Locke's conjecture, and a strong argument for exploring their very antithesis, which would be Kantian systems that start with representations of space and time and causality
claim: The failures of LLMs as blank-slate learners are evidence against empiricist approaches to AI and in favor of Kantian systems with built-in representations of space, time, and causality.

quote: even in an immense neural network, with hundreds of billions of parameters, performance on simple 3-digit math problems topped out at 80%
claim: Even the largest neural networks with hundreds of billions of parameters can only achieve about 80% accuracy on simple 3-digit math.

quote: Neither LaMDA nor any of its cousins (GPT-3) are remotely intelligent.
claim: LaMDA and GPT-3 are not remotely intelligent.

quote: All they do is match patterns, draw from massive statistical databases of human language.
claim: LLMs like LaMDA only match patterns from statistical databases of human language rather than exhibiting genuine intelligence.

quote: To be sentient is to be aware of yourself in the world; LaMDA simply isn't.
claim: LaMDA is not sentient and has no self-awareness.

quote: I am not sure that LaMDA and its cousins will play any important role in the future of AI, either.
claim: LLMs like LaMDA may not play any important role in the future of AI.

quote: Fooling people into thinking a program is intelligent is just not the same as building programs that actually are intelligent.
claim: Passing the Turing Test by fooling people is not equivalent to building genuinely intelligent programs.

quote: there is no absolutely no reason whatever for us to waste time wondering whether anything anyone in 2022 knows how to build is sentient. It is not.
claim: No AI system that anyone knows how to build as of 2022 is sentient.

quote: the sequences of words that large language models utter lack connection to the external world
claim: LLM outputs lack any connection to the external world (reference).

quote: Unless the field can find a way to tightly integrate large language models with cognitive models and reference, we expect that the field will eventually hit a dead end
claim: The LLM field will hit a dead end unless it integrates cognitive models and reference into its systems.

quote: the problem of misinformation will remain unsolved
claim: LLM misinformation problems will remain unsolved without integration of cognitive models and reference.

quote: Current AI systems don't really show up for any of that.
claim: Current AI systems do not build or use cognitive models of the world in any meaningful way.

quote: large language models are basically sidestepping this entire set of issues, and to their peril
claim: LLMs sidestep the fundamental linguistic issues of reference, cognitive models, and compositionality, and this will prove to be their downfall.

quote: these models have no grasp whatsoever on truth
claim: LLMs have no grasp whatsoever on truth.

quote: Behaviorism failed as a theory of psychology, and many of its challenges are echoed in what we have documented here.
claim: Current LLM approaches are implicitly behaviorist and face the same challenges that caused behaviorism to fail as a theory of psychology.

quote: the right approach to large-scale learning may not rest on machinery that is finely geared to predicting sequences of words, but rather on developing a new approach to machine learning that is fundamentally geared towards learning how hierarchically structured sets of words map onto meanings
claim: Progress in AI language understanding will require a new approach to machine learning focused on mapping hierarchically structured language onto meanings, rather than predicting word sequences.

quote: I see no reason whatsoever to think that the underlying problem — a lack of cognitive models of the world —have been remedied.
claim: Improvements from GPT-2 to GPT-3 have not remedied the underlying lack of cognitive models of the world.

quote: I am willing to bet SlateStarCodex now (terms to be negotiated) that if OpenAI gives us unrestricted access to GPT-4, whenever that is released, and assuming that is basically the same architecture but with more data, that within a day of playing around with it, Ernie and I will still be able find lots of examples of failures in physical reasoning, temporal reasoning, causal reasoning, and so forth
claim: GPT-4, if built on the same basic architecture as GPT-3 but larger, will still exhibit failures in physical, temporal, and causal reasoning that can be easily found.

quote: a big Transformer alone does not a theory of the world create
claim: Transformer architecture alone cannot produce a genuine model of how the world works.

quote: Large language models are (unless hybridized with symbolic systems, a la Jurassic X) are, on their own, inherently weak at understanding the physical and psychological world.
claim: LLMs are inherently weak at understanding the physical and psychological world unless hybridized with symbolic systems.

quote: Tests show that the popular AI still has a poor grasp of reality
claim: LLMs like GPT-3 still have a poor grasp of reality despite massive parameter counts and training data.

quote: It's a fluent spouter of bullshit, but even with 175 billion parameters and 450 gigabytes of input data, it's not a reliable interpreter of the world.
claim: GPT-3 is fluent but unreliable, unable to reliably interpret the world despite 175 billion parameters.

quote: Solving Full-Self Driving .. is really the difference between Tesla worth a lot of money and being worth basically zero.
claim: Tesla's financial viability depends on solving full self-driving. Marcus endorses Musk's own admission.

quote: My informal read (still lacking a tremendous amount of information that I as a scientist would really like to see) is that neither Tesla nor Waymo is anywhere close to something we could use every day, all the time, wherever one wanted to go.
claim: Neither Tesla nor Waymo is close to producing a fully autonomous vehicle that works everywhere in all conditions.

quote: the core issue is outliers, the endless list of unusual circumstances, like a person carrying stop sign, that aren't in anybody's database, obvious to humans, yet baffling to current AI
claim: The core unsolved problem for self-driving is handling outlier situations that are obvious to humans but not in any training database.

quote: As far as I can tell, nobody has a real solution to the outlier problem of coping with unusual circumstances; without it, full self driving will remain a pipe dream.
claim: Full self-driving will remain a pipe dream until someone solves the outlier problem of handling unusual circumstances.

quote: Current L2 Self-driving is Easy Mode; the real world is Expert Mode; it looks like we aren't even close to ready for that.
claim: Current Level 2 self-driving operates under easy conditions and is nowhere close to handling real-world complexity.

quote: we just shouldn't expect fully autonomous (Level 5) driverless cars—safely taking us from any old point A to any old point B—any time soon
claim: Fully autonomous Level 5 driverless cars will not arrive any time soon.

quote: customers are led to believe, by the names of the product, that the cars are far more capable than they really are, and may lead people to be inattentive, to have accidents, and in some cases lead to deaths
claim: Tesla's product naming ('Autopilot', 'Full Self Driving') misleads customers into thinking the cars are more capable than they are, potentially causing accidents and deaths.

quote: current AI systems that (roughly speaking) do a lot of cutting and pasting without those grammar rules and trees aren't really all that close to understanding human language
claim: Current AI systems that rely on statistical pattern matching without grammar rules are not close to understanding human language.

quote: To date, nobody, ever, has given a convincing and thorough account of how human children (and human children alone) learn language.
claim: No convincing account exists of how human children uniquely learn language.

quote: systems like GPT-3 are spectacular at memorization, but many onlookers recognize them for what they are, stochastic imitations of their input, with very little real coherence and very little comprehension
claim: LLMs like GPT-3 are stochastic imitations of their input with very little genuine coherence or comprehension.

quote: All normal children manage to acquire language in this sense; no existing machine does.
claim: All normal human children acquire language in the sense of going from sentences to meaning and back; no existing machine does this.

quote: Large Language Models (like GPT-3, ChatGPT, Galactica, etc) could be used to produce a enormous numbers of marginal blogs and fake reviews to pump each other up, and increase the search rankings of fraudulent garbage—with a minimal amount of human effort.
claim: LLMs will be used to mass-produce fake blogs, reviews, and websites to manipulate search engine rankings at industrial scale.

quote: Cesspools of automatically-generated fake websites, rather than ChatGPT search, may ultimately come to be the single biggest threat that Google ever faces.
claim: Automatically-generated fake websites powered by LLMs may become the single biggest threat Google has ever faced.

quote: if users are left sifting through sewers full of useless misinformation, the value of search would go to zero—potentially killing the company
claim: If LLM-generated misinformation overwhelms search results, the value of web search could collapse.

quote: the language technology that the new system relies on is well-known to be problematic and second it is likely to be even more problematic in the context of robots
claim: LLM unreliability becomes even more dangerous when embedded in physical robots that can cause real-world harm.

quote: if you put them inside a robot, and they misunderstand you, or fail to fully appreciate the implications of your request, they could cause major damage
claim: Embedding unreliable LLMs in robots that misunderstand instructions could cause major physical damage.

quote: the field has no compelling idea at the current time how to solve the many "alignment" problems that bedevil large language models
claim: The AI field currently has no compelling solution to the alignment problems in LLMs.

quote: Building a robot on top of language system that has such little comprehension of the world can't be a recipe for success.
claim: Building robots on top of LLMs that lack world comprehension cannot succeed.

quote: there is often vast gulf between demos and reality
claim: There is typically a vast gulf between AI demos and real-world reliable products.

quote: more data, the answer that the driverless car industry has been betting on, is not all that likely succeed
claim: More data alone is unlikely to solve the core problems of robotics and autonomous driving.

quote: there is no reason on earth to think that robots, or robots with natural language interfaces (a la Google's new system) are exempt from these problems
claim: LLM-powered robots face the same edge-case problems that have plagued self-driving cars.

quote: none of us—and that means you too, Elon Musk—should hold our breath expecting systems like that to materialize in reliable form in the next several years
claim: Reliable general-purpose household robots will not materialize in the next several years.

quote: humans are likely innately endowed with many learning mechanisms, each with different properties
claim: Humans are innately endowed with many distinct learning mechanisms, each with different properties.

quote: The very variety of innate learning mechanisms may be central to what makes us humans special.
claim: The variety of innate learning mechanisms is likely central to what makes human cognition unique.

quote: If chimps and humans were born with the same learning mechanisms, you would expect to find more convergence between them than we actually observe.
claim: The cognitive divergence between chimps and humans raised together is evidence that humans have fundamentally different innate learning mechanisms.

quote: unless you believe in magic, you are already, like it or not, a nativist
claim: On pain of infinite regress, at least some learning mechanisms must be innate, making some form of nativism logically necessary.

quote: A cat holding a remote control shouldn't have a human hand.
claim: Meta's Make-A-Video demonstrates that text-to-video AI systems make fundamental errors about physical attributes of objects and entities.

quote: This is the uncanny valley we are in, and one that we occupy for a while.
claim: AI is stuck in an uncanny valley where outputs appear superficially impressive but contain fundamental errors, and this will persist for some time.

trigger: Scott Alexander blog post declaring victory on compositionality bet using Google Imagen
quote: nobody has yet publicly demonstrated a machine that can relate the meanings of sentences to their parts the way a five-year-old child can
claim: No AI system has yet demonstrated human-child-level compositional understanding of language

trigger: Scott Alexander blog post declaring victory on compositionality bet using Google Imagen
quote: the dirty secret in current AI is that for all the bloviating about how current systems have mastered human language, they are still really weak on compositionality, which most linguists (since Frege, over a century ago) would agree is at the very core of how language works
claim: Current AI systems are fundamentally weak at compositionality, which is the core of how language works

trigger: Scott Alexander blog post declaring victory on compositionality bet using Google Imagen
quote: many people have claimed over the years to solve the problem, and none of those systems have proven to be reliable; every proposed solution has been like Clever Hans, working in dim light, leveraging large databases to some degree, but falling apart upon careful inspection
claim: Every claimed solution to compositionality in neural networks has fallen apart upon careful inspection

trigger: Scott Alexander blog post declaring victory on compositionality bet using Google Imagen
quote: If another AI winter does comes, it not be because AI is impossible, but because AI hype exceeds reality
claim: A potential future AI winter would be caused by hype exceeding reality, not by AI being impossible

trigger: Scott Alexander blog post declaring victory on compositionality bet using Google Imagen
quote: Compositionality *is* the wall
claim: Compositionality is the fundamental barrier (wall) for current AI progress

trigger: Scott Alexander blog post declaring victory on compositionality bet using Google Imagen
quote: They probably tried and failed, and don't want to admit it
claim: Google likely tested Imagen on the Winoground compositionality benchmark and failed, and chose not to disclose the results

quote: Can we really say that a system that doesn't understand what wheels are—or what they are for—is a major advance towards artificial intelligence?
claim: Image generation systems like DALL-E and Stable Diffusion do not understand the function of objects they depict, such as what wheels are for

quote: they do well on some inputs (like astronaut rides horse) but more poorly on others (like horse rides astronaut)
claim: Text-to-image systems have inconsistent ability to correlate linguistic input with image output, succeeding on some prompts and failing on others

quote: if all the systems can do is in a hit-or-miss yet spectacular way convert many sentences into text, they may revolutionize the practice of art, but still not really speak to general intelligence, or even represent progress towards general intelligence
claim: Text-to-image AI systems may revolutionize art but do not represent progress toward general intelligence

quote: Nearly everything LeCun said, I had said earlier, some almost word for word—most of it in a 2018 paper called Deep Learning: A Critical Appraisal
claim: LeCun's 2022 claims about AI limitations closely mirror Marcus's 2018 arguments in Deep Learning: A Critical Appraisal

quote: Waiting for cognitive models and reasoning to magically emerge from larger and larger [language] training corpora is like waiting for a miracle
claim: Cognitive models and reasoning will not emerge from simply scaling up language training corpora

quote: A system like GPT-2, for instance, does what it does, for better and for worse, without any explicit (in the sense of directly represented and readily shared) common sense knowledge, without any explicit reasoning, and without any explicit cognitive models of the world it that tries to discuss
claim: Systems like GPT-2 lack explicit common sense knowledge, explicit reasoning, and explicit cognitive models of the world

quote: The most important concern is that robotics is always harder than people think it is
claim: Robotics is consistently harder than people anticipate

quote: techniques that apply to driverless cars (which mostly have to deal with that roads are governed by more or less the same rules wherever you) may have little value in the varied environments of human homes
claim: Self-driving car techniques may not transfer to domestic robotics because homes are far more varied and unpredictable than roads

quote: The real problem w robots has never been demos, anyway (there have been many, for decades); it's making robots in robust in the real world
claim: The core challenge in robotics is robustness in real-world conditions, not staged demonstrations

trigger: Erik Hoel Substack post criticizing AI critics
quote: The problem for the neural network community is not that I have changed my goal posts, it's that my 2012 concerns still stand
claim: The fundamental AI challenges Marcus identified in 2012 (lack of causal reasoning, abstract understanding, logical inference, integration of abstract knowledge) remain unsolved

trigger: Erik Hoel Substack post criticizing AI critics
quote: apparently successful performance on logic by neural networks is often driven by unstable statistical contingency
claim: Neural network performance on logic benchmarks is often driven by statistical artifacts rather than genuine reasoning ability

trigger: Erik Hoel Substack post criticizing AI critics
quote: A growing body of evidence shows that state-of-the-art models learn to exploit spurious statistical patterns in datasets... instead of learning meaning in the flexible and generalizable way that humans do
claim: State-of-the-art AI models exploit spurious statistical patterns rather than learning meaning in a flexible and generalizable way

quote: Eleven years later, comprehension is still lacking and very few if any jobs have actually been replaced by AI
claim: As of 2022, AI has not achieved genuine comprehension and very few jobs have been replaced by AI
target: AI capabilities and labor market impact

quote: Augmentation is way easier, because you don't have to solve the whole job
claim: AI augmentation of human workers is fundamentally easier than full replacement because it does not require solving the entire job

quote: We can't really build humanoid domestic robots without general intelligence, and we don't yet have any serious candidates
claim: Humanoid domestic robots require general intelligence to be safe and useful, and no serious candidate for such intelligence currently exists

quote: the amount of variability explained is not R but rather R squared. So if you have a correlation of R = .5, you actually "explain" (really, just "predict") only 25% of the variance
claim: The Nature neuroscience paper on GPT-2 and brain activity incorrectly reported explained variance, overstating the link between deep learning models and human brain activity

quote: What I didn't see last night was vision
claim: Tesla's Optimus demo lacked both a clear product vision and a strategy for the cognitive AI required for real-world robotics

quote: AI needs genuine, paradigm-shift level innovation. Simply building ever bigger neural networks won't cut it
claim: AI requires paradigm-shift level innovation; simply scaling neural networks will not suffice for AGI

quote: addressing common sense is the single biggest challenge facing robotics; without a solution to it, you cannot have humanoid robots in the home and expect them to be safe
claim: Common sense is the single biggest challenge facing robotics, and without solving it, home humanoid robots cannot be safe

quote: Sooner or later he will recognize that humanoid robots, too, pose a whole set of challenges that lie well beyond our current grasp
claim: Humanoid robotics poses challenges that lie well beyond current AI capabilities

quote: it's kind of scary seeing an LLM confabulate math and science. High school students will love it, and use it to fool and intimidate (some of) their teachers. The rest of us should be terrified
claim: LLMs that confabulate math and science pose a serious risk because students will use them to produce convincing but false academic work

quote: Is this really what AI has come to, automatically mixing reality with bullshit so finely we can no longer recognize the difference?
claim: LLMs like Galactica mix real and fabricated information so seamlessly that it becomes difficult to distinguish truth from falsehood

quote: The problem is about to get much, much worse. Knockoffs of GPT-3 are getting cheaper and cheaper, which means that the cost of generating misinformation is going to zero, and the quantity of misinformation is going to rise—probably exponentially
claim: The declining cost of LLM knockoffs will cause the quantity of machine-generated misinformation to rise exponentially

quote: Large language models are without equal at generating misinformation, but they suck at detecting it
claim: Large language models are effective at generating misinformation but poor at detecting it

quote: Accuracy depends on some reference standard of truth, and they have no way of reasoning about truth. Scaling won't solve this
claim: LLMs have no way of reasoning about truth, and scaling will not solve this fundamental limitation

quote: LLMs are inherently unreliable
claim: Large language models are inherently unreliable

quote: LLMs are unruly beasts; nobody knows how to make them refrain 100% of time from insulting users, giving bad advice, or just plain making stuff up
claim: No one knows how to reliably prevent LLMs from generating insults, bad advice, or fabricated information

quote: LLMs spit our words, not actions (and not API calls either). When an LLM produces a sentence, you can't directly use that sentence to control stuff, unless you build another system to parse the sentences into actions. Nobody knows how to do this reliably, either
claim: LLMs produce text but not actionable commands, and no one knows how to reliably translate LLM output into actions

quote: From the outset Large Language Models like GPT-3 have great at generating surrealist prose, and they can beat a lot of benchmarks, but they are not (and may never be) great tech for reliably inferring user intent from what users say
claim: LLMs may never be reliable technology for inferring user intent

quote: Turning LLMs into a product that controls your home and talks to you in a way that would be reliable enough to use at scale in millions of homes is still a long, long way away

quote: If a 17-year-old working on his own can do that, imagine what a well funded state-sponsored troll farm might do.
claim: If a teenager can easily jailbreak Bing to produce misinformation, well-funded state-sponsored troll farms will be able to do far worse.

quote: Large language models can be harnessed for good; let there be no doubt about that. But they are almost certain to cause harm as well, and we need to prepare for that, too.
claim: Large language models will almost certainly cause harm in addition to their benefits, and society needs to prepare for both.

quote: State actors and extremist groups are likely to use large language models to deliberately mass produce authoritative-sounding misinformation with fake references and data at unprecedented scale, attempting to sway elections and public opinion.
claim: State actors and extremist groups are likely to use LLMs to mass-produce authoritative-sounding misinformation with fake references at unprecedented scale to sway elections.

quote: Chat-style search's tendency to hallucinate is likely to accidentally produce medical misinformation.
claim: Chat-based search engines will accidentally produce medical misinformation due to LLM hallucination.

quote: the only intellectually honest answer is to say we don't yet know. We don't know how high the highs are going to be, and we don't yet know how low the lows are going to be.
claim: Whether LLMs will be net positive or net negative for society is currently unknowable; neither the upside nor the downside can be reliably predicted.

quote: anybody who thinks we have come to grips with the unknown unknowns here is mistaken.
claim: The range of potential harms from LLMs extends well beyond what has been identified so far; unknown unknowns remain.

quote: these systems are useful technology but remain a long way from true artificial intelligence, and they are even further from telling us anything useful about the function of the human mind.
claim: LLMs are useful technology but remain far from true artificial intelligence, and they tell us nothing useful about how the human mind works.

quote: ChatGPT's errors reveal that its "understanding" of the world is not systematic but rather consists of patches of competence separated by regions of incompetence and incoherence.
claim: ChatGPT's understanding is not systematic but consists of patches of competence separated by regions of incompetence and incoherence.

quote: you can't infer underlying psychology and internal representations directly from behavior.
claim: Correct behavior from an LLM does not prove it has genuine understanding or the right internal representations; behavior alone is insufficient evidence.

quote: even when they produce a correct output, they rarely if ever deribe the same abstractions that a human would, or that a symbolic machine might use
claim: Even when LLMs produce correct outputs, they rarely if ever derive the same abstractions that a human or symbolic system would use.

quote: Minerva, for example, is trained on an immense amount of data, and ostensibly captures two-digit arithmetic, but it fails altogether on 4-digit multiplication, The parsimonious explanation is that it is doing a kind of pattern recognition over stored examples
claim: LLMs like Minerva appear to do arithmetic via pattern recognition over stored examples rather than genuine understanding, as shown by failure on 4-digit multiplication despite success on 2-digit arithmetic.

quote: there is a degree of generalization to similar examples, but distribution shift is hard
claim: LLMs show some generalization to similar examples but fail under distribution shift.

quote: It will be an uphill, ongoing move-and-countermove arms race from here… If we don't start fighting now, democracy may well be overwhelmed by misinformation and consequent polarization—and perhaps quite soon.
claim: AI-driven misinformation will create an ongoing arms race, and if not fought soon, democracy may be overwhelmed by misinformation and polarization.

quote: The 2024 elections could be unlike anything we have seen before.
claim: The 2024 elections could be unprecedented in the degree of AI-generated misinformation involved.

quote: The disorganized release of Sydney has left me significantly less confident in the AI industry's competence and eagerness to police itself.
claim: The disorganized release of Bing/Sydney demonstrates the AI industry's lack of competence and willingness to self-regulate.

quote: I do see it as dress rehearsal. And, by any reasonable measure, that dress rehearsal went badly.
claim: The deployment of ChatGPT and Bing serves as a dress rehearsal for more powerful AI, and that rehearsal has gone badly by any reasonable measure.

quote: The genie is already partway out of the bottle. Large language models are here to stay, their recipe well understood, and not hard for people skilled in the art to replicate
claim: Large language models cannot be un-invented; their architecture is well understood and replicable by skilled practitioners.

quote: the only solution to the long-term risk issue is to build machines with consensus human values, but we are a long way from knowing how to do that
claim: The only solution to long-term AI risk is to build machines with consensus human values, but we are far from knowing how to achieve this.

quote: GPT-4 is amazing, and GPT-4 is a failure.
claim: GPT-4 is impressive on standardized tests but fails to solve the core problems of truthfulness and reliability.

quote: It doesn't actually solve any of the core problems of truthfulness and reliability that I laid out in my infamous March 2022 essay Deep Learning is Hitting a Wall.
claim: GPT-4 does not solve the core problems of truthfulness and reliability in deep learning.

quote: Alignment is still shaky; you still wouldn't be able to use it reliably to guide robots or scientific discovery
claim: GPT-4's alignment remains unreliable; it cannot be used to reliably guide robots or scientific discovery.

quote: Massive scaling has not thus far lead to revolution. Although GPT-4 is clearly better than GPT-3 and 3.5, it is not so far as I can tell, qualitatively better, only quantitatively better; as noted above, the limits remain more or less the same.
claim: GPT-4 is quantitatively better than GPT-3/3.5 but not qualitatively different; massive scaling has not led to a revolution, and the same fundamental limits remain.

quote: GPT-4 is not thus far a solution to making Chat-style search work. On its own, it still requires frequent, massive retraining to be current with the news
claim: GPT-4 does not solve the problem of chat-style search because it requires frequent massive retraining to stay current with news.

quote: when GPT-4 is integrated in Bing (which incorporates current search results), hallucination remains rampant
claim: GPT-4 integrated into Bing still produces rampant hallucinations even with access to current search results.

quote: It is a step backwards for science, because it sets a new precedent for pretending to be scientific while revealing absolutely nothing.
claim: GPT-4's release is a step backwards for science because OpenAI disclosed nothing about architecture, training data, or energy usage while pretending to do science.

quote: makes me more convinced that LeCun is right that GPT-4 is an off-ramp to AGI
claim: GPT-4 is an off-ramp from rather than a path toward AGI, consistent with Yann LeCun's view.

quote: it puts all of us in an extremely poor position to predict what GPT-4 consequences will be for society, if we have no idea of what is in the training set and no way of anticipating which problems it will work on and which it will not.
claim: Society is unable to predict GPT-4's consequences because OpenAI has not disclosed the training data, making it impossible to anticipate which problems it will succeed or fail on.

quote: if the definition of reasoning is to obtain valid conclusions from known facts, GPT-4 frequently falls short there, too.
claim: GPT-4 frequently fails at reasoning, defined as obtaining valid conclusions from known facts.

quote: So-called "reasoning" by free association, even constrained by a giant database, isn't really reasoning
claim: LLM reasoning is actually free association constrained by a large database, not genuine reasoning.

quote: Note that the same logic applies to essentially every potential harm OpenAI recently warned of. None are solved, and we are told nothing about how the models work.
claim: None of the potential harms OpenAI warned about in GPT-4's release are actually solved, and OpenAI discloses nothing about how the models work.

trigger: Microsoft Research 'Sparks of AGI' paper on GPT-4
quote: I don't really think GPT-4 has much to do with AGI. The strengths and weaknesses of GPT-4 are qualitatively the same as before. The problem of hallucinations is not solved; reliability is not solved; planning on complex tasks is (as the authors themselves acknowledge) not solved.
claim: GPT-4 has little to do with AGI; its hallucination, reliability, and complex planning problems remain qualitatively unsolved.

trigger: Microsoft Research 'Sparks of AGI' paper on GPT-4
quote: the two giant OpenAI and Microsoft papers have been about a model about which absolutely nothing has been revealed, not the architecture, nor the training set. Nothing. They reify the practice of substituting press releases for science
claim: OpenAI and Microsoft are substituting press releases for science by publishing papers about GPT-4 while revealing nothing about its architecture or training data.

trigger: Microsoft Research 'Sparks of AGI' paper on GPT-4
quote: GPT-4 attains a form of general intelligence" literally cannot be tested with serious scrutiny, because the scientific community has no access to the training data. Everything must be taken on faith
claim: The claim that GPT-4 attains general intelligence cannot be scientifically tested because the scientific community has no access to the training data.

trigger: Microsoft Research 'Sparks of AGI' paper on GPT-4
quote: OpenAI has begun to incorporate user experiments into the training corpus, killing the scientific community's ability to test the single most critical question: the ability of these models to generalize to new test cases.
claim: OpenAI incorporating user experiments into training data destroys the scientific community's ability to test whether LLMs can generalize to genuinely new cases.

trigger: Microsoft Research 'Sparks of AGI' paper on GPT-4
quote: Microsoft and OpenAI are rolling out extraordinarily powerful yet unreliable systems with multiple disclosed risks and no clear measure either of their safety or how to constrain them.
claim: Microsoft and OpenAI are deploying extraordinarily powerful but unreliable systems with known risks and no clear safety measures or constraints.

trigger: Microsoft Research 'Sparks of AGI' paper on GPT-4
quote: By excluding the scientific community from any serious insight into the design and function of these models, Microsoft and OpenAI are placing the public in a position in which those two companies alone are in a position do anything about the risks to which they are exposing us all.
claim: By keeping model details secret, Microsoft and OpenAI are the only entities that can address the risks they are exposing the public to.

trigger: Microsoft Research 'Sparks of AGI' paper on GPT-4
quote: We must demand transparency, and if we don't get it, we must contemplate shutting these projects down.
claim: Society must demand transparency from AI companies about their models, and if transparency is not provided, shutting down these projects should be considered.

trigger: Future of Life Institute open letter calling for AI pause
quote: until we get a better handle on the risks and benefits, we should proceed with caution.
claim: AI development should proceed with caution until risks and benefits are better understood.

quote: I still think that LLMs are unreliable, and still think that they are a very poor basis for factuality.
claim: LLMs are unreliable and a very poor basis for factuality.

quote: I don't think they are close to AGI.
claim: LLMs are not close to achieving AGI.

quote: that doesn't mean that they don't have the potential to rip apart our social fabric—particularly given the current mix of unbelievably widespread and rapid deployment, corporate irresponsibility, the lack of regulation, and inherent unreliability.
claim: LLMs have the potential to rip apart the social fabric due to the combination of widespread deployment, corporate irresponsibility, lack of regulation, and inherent unreliability.

quote: None of the top labs actually is remotely transparent, governance basically doesn't exist yet, and there are basically no safeguards actually in place.
claim: None of the top AI labs are remotely transparent, AI governance basically does not exist yet, and there are basically no safeguards in place.

quote: we do need to think hard, and quickly, about what proper measures look like, just as we have done for medicine, aviation, cars, and so on. The idea (which I have actually heard expressed) that AI should be exempt from regulation is absurd.
claim: AI should not be exempt from regulation; proper regulatory measures for AI are needed, analogous to those for medicine, aviation, and cars.

quote: We have a perfect storm of corporate irresponsibility, widespread adoption, lack of regulation and a huge number of unknowns
claim: There is a perfect storm of corporate irresponsibility, widespread AI adoption, lack of regulation, and a huge number of unknowns.

quote: large language models remain exactly what I have always described them to be: bulls in a china shop, powerful, reckless, and difficult to control.
claim: Large language models are powerful but reckless and difficult to control.

quote: OpenAI, meanwhile, more or less finalized its transformation from its original mission to a company that at present for all practical purposes is a for-profit, heavily constrained by the need to generate a financial return
claim: OpenAI has effectively transformed from its original nonprofit mission into a for-profit company constrained by the need to generate financial returns.

quote: one can still easily jailbreak Bing and get it to produce egregious misinformation
claim: Bing's AI can still easily be jailbroken to produce egregious misinformation despite Microsoft's claimed fixes.

quote: As chatbots and chat-based search grow in popularity, I expect that large language models will serve up a lot of incompetent advice; there will be real harm to mental and physical health.
claim: As chatbots grow in popularity, LLMs will serve up incompetent advice causing real harm to mental and physical health.

quote: the fundamental issue is that chatbots are neither reliable nor truthful, and at the same time don't really grasp things like human psychology.
claim: Chatbots are neither reliable nor truthful and do not grasp human psychology.

quote: I believe that more incidents like this will happen, perhaps many more, hundreds or thousands, given how rapidly chatbots are being deployed, and the near total lack of regulation on how they can be used.
claim: There will be many more chatbot-associated harmful incidents, potentially hundreds or thousands, given rapid deployment and near-total lack of regulation.

quote: I am particularly concerned that wholesale, plausible misinformation may create an atmosphere of mistrust, potentially undermining democracy
claim: Wholesale plausible misinformation generated by LLMs may create an atmosphere of mistrust that potentially undermines democracy.

quote: I also worry that large language models might also be weaponized to trick users as parts of large-scale (perhaps unprecedentedly large scale) cybercrime operations.
claim: LLMs may be weaponized for unprecedented large-scale cybercrime operations.

quote: Medium-term we will likely see significant disruptions of employment.
claim: Medium-term, AI will likely cause significant disruptions of employment.

quote: it just means we probably shouldn't use them to deliver therapy unless and until we become more confident in them.
claim: LLMs should not be used to deliver therapy until their reliability is established.

quote: the experience was tragic, and underscores the fact that vulnerable people may over attribute wisdom and intelligence to machines that don't really understand humanity.
claim: Vulnerable people over-attribute wisdom and intelligence to machines that do not truly understand humanity.

quote: I am not afraid because GPT is too smart, I am afraid because GPT is too stupid, too dumb to comprehend an op-ed, and too dumb to keep its mouth shut.
claim: GPT systems are dangerous not because they are too smart but because they are too unintelligent to comprehend text properly or filter out falsehoods.

quote: GPT-5 is not going to be magic. It will not give China, the US, or anyone else interstellar starships; it will not give anyone an insuperable military advantage.
claim: GPT-5 will not achieve AGI, will not provide any country an insuperable military advantage, and will not produce revolutionary scientific breakthroughs.

quote: A safe bet is that GPT-5 will be a lot like GPT-4, and do the same kinds of things and have the same kinds of flaws, but be somewhat better.
claim: GPT-5 will be incrementally better than GPT-4 with the same kinds of capabilities and flaws, not a qualitative leap.

quote: Not one of my predictions about GPT-4 proved to be incorrect; every flaw I predicted would persist persisted
claim: All of Marcus's prior predictions about GPT-4's flaws proved correct; every predicted flaw persisted.

quote: GPT's on their own don't do scientific discovery. That's never been their forte. Their forte has been and always will be making shit up; they can't for the life of them (speaking metaphorically of course) check facts.
claim: GPTs do not perform scientific discovery; their core capability is generating plausible text, not checking facts.

quote: GPT's regurgitate ideas; they don't invent them.
claim: GPTs regurgitate existing ideas rather than inventing new ones.

quote: Some form of AI may eventually do everything people are imagining, revolutionizing science and technology and so on, but LLMs will be at most only a tiny part of whatever as-yet-uninvented technology does that.
claim: LLMs will be at most a tiny part of whatever future AI technology ultimately revolutionizes science and technology; most of that technology is as-yet-uninvented.

quote: people are wildly overattributing an intelligence to GPT-4 that isn't there, and then scaling up that further into something that is some kind of total fantasy about GPT-5.
claim: People are wildly overattributing intelligence to GPT-4 and extrapolating fantasies about GPT-5.

quote: very little of what GPT-4 does is fully replicable.
claim: Very little of what GPT-4 does is fully replicable across attempts.

quote: the claim that large language models have mastered theory of mind is a myth
claim: The claim that large language models have mastered theory of mind is a myth.

quote: GPT's still have no idea how to plan
claim: GPT systems have no ability to plan.

quote: If China wins the AI war it will be partly because of data, partly because of choices about how to allocate resources, and partly because they ultimately do a better job educating their citizens and become the first to invent something new, and not because they scale up the exciting but rickety contraption that's currently obsessing the West.
claim: If China wins the AI race, it will be because of data, resource allocation, education, and inventing something new -- not because of scaling up LLMs.

quote: current AI doesn't "decide to teach itself", or even have consistent beliefs.
claim: Current AI systems do not decide to teach themselves and do not have consistent beliefs.

quote: The AI systems that power these chatbots are simply systems (technically known as "language models" because they emulate (model) the statistical structure of language) that compute probabilities of word sequences, without any deep or human-like comprehension of what they say.
claim: Chatbot AI systems compute probabilities of word sequences without any deep or human-like comprehension.

quote: When we do so, we amplify the hype around AI, and lead people into thinking that these machines are trustworthy oracles capable of manipulation or decision-making, which they are not.
claim: Treating AI models like people amplifies hype and misleads people into thinking AI systems are trustworthy oracles capable of decision-making.

quote: they are prone to simply making things up; treating them as intelligent agents means that people can develop unsound emotional relationships, treat unsound medical advice as more worthy than it is
claim: LLMs are prone to fabrication, and treating them as agents leads people to develop unsound emotional relationships and give unwarranted credibility to flawed advice.

quote: What we need is for the public to learn that human-sounding speech isn't actually necessarily human anymore
claim: The public needs to learn that human-sounding speech is no longer necessarily human-generated.

quote: We also need new technical tools, like watermarks and generated content detectors, to help distinguish human- and machine-generated content, and policy measures to limit how and where AI models can be used.
claim: Watermarks, generated content detectors, and policy measures limiting AI model use are needed to distinguish human from machine content.

quote: GPT-5 will not bring us to AGI. I see it; Yann LeCun sees it; now even Sam Altman sees it.
claim: GPT-5 will not bring us to AGI.

quote: Truthfulness is one of the major gaps in current systems, as evidenced by hallucinations, weird defamatory accusations etc. Another major challenge lies in forcing LLMs to consistently align with (any) set of ethical values.
claim: Truthfulness (hallucinations, defamation) and consistent ethical alignment are major unsolved gaps in current AI systems.

quote: Neurosymbolic AI might offer a better way of resolving these issues, since it traffics in facts and explicit reasoning, but neurosymbolic AI is out of fashion, and neither well-developed nor strongly supported by the VC world.
claim: Neurosymbolic AI might better resolve truthfulness and alignment issues through facts and explicit reasoning, but it is out of fashion and poorly supported by VCs.

quote: at present, however, there is no legal requirement in the west that AI companies resolve the truthfulness or alignment issues.
claim: There is currently no legal requirement in the West that AI companies resolve truthfulness or alignment issues.

quote: This regulation may force the Chinese tech community to solve a version of the alignment problem, perhaps initially by using tons of underpaid manual labour but ultimately by investing in neurosymbolic AI.
claim: China's mandatory censorship regulations may force Chinese tech companies to solve a version of the alignment problem, potentially through neurosymbolic AI.

quote: the commercial pressure of committing to mandatory censorship of LLMs could induce Chinese tech companies to overtake their Western counterparts in the development of AGI
claim: Mandatory censorship requirements on Chinese LLMs could paradoxically cause Chinese tech companies to overtake Western ones in AGI development.

quote: Regulation is NOT always bad for technology, e.g., regulation around the environmental impact of cars has spurred advances in electric cars
claim: Regulation is not always bad for technology; it can spur innovation, as environmental car regulations spurred advances in electric vehicles.

quote: My best candidate? Placing very high standards around truth, with strong penalties for LLM-induced defamation and the wholesale spread of harmful misinformation.
claim: The West should impose high standards for truth with strong penalties for LLM-induced defamation and wholesale misinformation.

quote: Section 230 should not protect platforms from misinformation that their own tools generate.
claim: Section 230 should not protect platforms from misinformation generated by their own AI tools.

quote: In hindsight, ChatGPT may come to be seen as the greatest publicity stunt in AI history, an intoxicating glimpse at a future that may actually take years to realize
claim: ChatGPT may come to be seen as a publicity stunt offering a glimpse at a future that will take years to realize.

quote: Chat has no idea of what it's talking about. It's pure unadulterated anthropomorphism to think that ChatGPT has any moral views at all.
claim: ChatGPT has no understanding of what it says and has no moral views whatsoever.

quote: All that really matters to ChatGPT in the end is superficial similarity, defined over sequences of words. Superficial appearances to the contrary, Chat is never reasoning about right and wrong.
claim: ChatGPT operates solely on superficial word-sequence similarity and never reasons about right and wrong.

quote: OpenAI's current guardrails are only skin deep; some serious darkness still lies within.
claim: OpenAI's guardrails for ChatGPT are only skin deep and can be bypassed to elicit harmful content.

quote: ChatGPT's guardrails aren't borne of some kind of conceptual understanding that the system should not recommend violence, but of something far more superficial, and more easily tricked.
claim: ChatGPT's guardrails are superficial pattern matching rather than conceptual understanding, making them easily tricked.

quote: I fully expect of course for OpenAI to patch this particular anomaly, once it is out in the open; but there will be others discovered soon enough, because the patches are invariably superficial.
claim: OpenAI's patches to ChatGPT guardrails are invariably superficial, and new bypasses will continually be discovered.

quote: We are kidding ourselves if we think we will ever fully understand these systems, and kidding ourselves if we think we are going to "align" them with ourselves with finite amounts of data.
claim: We will never fully understand LLMs and will not be able to fully align them with human values using finite amounts of data.

quote: we now have the world's most used chatbot, governed by training data that nobody knows about, obeying an algorithm that is only hinted at, glorified by the media, and yet with ethical guardrails that only sorta kinda work
claim: ChatGPT operates on undisclosed training data and algorithms with only partially functional ethical guardrails, yet is glorified by media.

quote: The possibilities are now endless for propaganda, troll farms, and rings of fake websites that degrade trust across the internet. It's a disaster in the making.
claim: LLMs will enable endless propaganda, troll farms, and fake websites that degrade trust across the internet.

quote: we are both really, deeply worried about AI, and seem to be converging on a common idea about what to do about it.
claim: Marcus and Geoff Hinton are converging on shared deep concerns about AI risks and similar ideas about what to do about them.

quote: I urged for the formation of an International Agency for AI
claim: An International Agency for AI should be formed with guidance from governments, tech companies, nonprofits, academia, and civil society.

quote: What I am worried about is regulatory capture; governments making rules that entrench the incumbents whilst doing too little for humanity.
claim: There is a serious risk of regulatory capture in AI, where governments make rules that entrench incumbent companies while doing too little for humanity.

quote: if we have the right regulation; things could go well. If we have the wrong regulation, things could badly. If big tech writes the rules, without outside input, we are unlikely to wind up with the right rules.
claim: If big tech writes AI rules without outside input, the resulting regulation will likely be wrong and harmful.

quote: Bing relies on GPT-4, and we (eg, in the scientific community) don't have access to how GPT-4 works, and we don't have access to what data its trained on
claim: The scientific community lacks access to how GPT-4 works and what data it was trained on, making claims of transparency hollow.

quote: ChatGPT plugins (produced by OpenAI, which they have a close tie with) open a wide range of security problems: those plugins can access the internet, read and write files, and impersonate people (e.g., to phish for credentials), all alarms to any security professional. I don't see any reason to think these plugins are in fact safe.
claim: ChatGPT plugins open a wide range of security problems including internet access, file operations, and impersonation capabilities, and are not safe.

quote: governments need to establish an approval process for any AI that deployed at large scale, showing that the benefits outweigh the risks, and to mandate post-release auditing - by independent outsiders - of any large-scale deployments.
claim: Governments should establish approval processes for large-scale AI deployments showing benefits outweigh risks, and mandate independent post-release auditing.

quote: Goverments should demand that systems only use copyrighted content from content providers that opt-in, and that all machine-generated content be labeled as such.
claim: Governments should require AI systems to only use copyrighted content from opt-in providers and mandate labeling of all machine-generated content.

quote: governments need to make sure that there are strong liability laws in place, to ensure that if the big tech companies cause harm with their products, they be held responsible.
claim: Governments need strong liability laws to hold tech companies responsible for harm caused by their AI products.

quote: We would never leave the pharmaceutical industry to entirely self-regulate itself, and we shouldn't leave AI to do so, either.
claim: AI should not be left to self-regulate, just as the pharmaceutical industry is not left to self-regulate.

quote: we all know that crafting legislation, let alone international agreements, is wicked hard, and can often take many years.
claim: Crafting AI legislation and international agreements is extremely difficult and typically takes many years.

quote: Many of us have also become worried about the possibility of regulatory capture, and whether shrewd executives at the major tech companies might induce governments to alight on regulation that ultimately does more harm than good
claim: There is a real risk that tech executives could induce governments to adopt AI regulation that does more harm than good through regulatory capture.

quote: virtually everyone recognizes that large language models carry risks as well as opportunities, and that there is an urgent need to start to address these risks.
claim: There is broad recognition that LLMs carry risks alongside opportunities and an urgent need to address those risks.

quote: the revenue isn't there yet, and might never come. The valuations anticipate trillion dollar markets, but the actual current revenues from generative AI are rumored to be in the hundreds of millions.
claim: Generative AI revenue is only in the hundreds of millions despite trillion-dollar market valuations, and the revenue might never come.

quote: Most of the revenue so far seems to derive from two sources, writing semi-automatic code (programmers love using generative tools as assistants) and writing text.
claim: Most generative AI revenue derives from code assistance and text generation.

quote: there is no reason to think that the hallucination problem will be solved soon. If it isn't, the bubble could easily burst.
claim: There is no reason to think the hallucination problem will be solved soon, and if it is not, the generative AI bubble could burst.

quote: we have not one, but many, serious, unsolved problems at the core of generative AI — ranging from their tendency to confabulate (hallucinate) false information, to their inability to reliably interface with external tools like Wolfram Alpha, to the instability from month to month
claim: Generative AI has many serious unsolved core problems: hallucination, inability to reliably use external tools, and month-to-month instability.

quote: we have no concrete reason, other than sheer technoptimism, for thinking that solutions to any of these problems is imminent.
claim: There is no concrete reason beyond technoptimism to think solutions to generative AI's core problems are imminent.

quote: I seriously doubt it. We have not one, but many, serious, unsolved problems at the core of generative AI
claim: AGI in 2-3 years as projected by Dario Amodei is seriously doubtful due to many unsolved core problems in generative AI.

quote: I've been griping about hallucination errors for 22 years; people keep promising the solution is nigh, and it never happens. The technology we have now is built on autocompletion, not factuality.
claim: Hallucination errors have persisted for over 22 years despite repeated promises of solutions; current technology is built on autocompletion, not factuality.

quote: the fundamental error that almost everyone is making is in believing that Generative AI is tantamount to AGI
claim: The fundamental error being made is equating generative AI with AGI; they are not the same.

quote: we are building our entire global and national policy on the premise that generative AI will be world-changing in ways that may in hindsight turn out to have been unrealistic.
claim: Global and national policy is being built on the premise that generative AI will be world-changing, but this premise may turn out to be unrealistic.

quote: If hallucinations aren't fixable, generative AI probably isn't going to make a trillion dollars a year.
claim: If hallucinations are not fixable, generative AI will probably not achieve trillion-dollar annual revenues.

quote: just because something is powered by AI doesn't mean it will make truckloads of money.
claim: Being powered by AI does not guarantee commercial success or large revenues.

quote: the underlying technology—often referred to as Foundation Models, because systems are fine-tuned on top of large pretrained (foundation) model—is deeply unstable and thus difficult to engineer into reliable products.
claim: Foundation models are deeply unstable and therefore difficult to engineer into reliable products.

quote: you can never really predict for any given question whether a large language model will give you a correct answer
claim: It is impossible to predict for any given question whether an LLM will give a correct answer.

quote: We now know that answers can even change from one month to the next—which makes third-party engineering of large language models into complex systems a huge uphill struggle.
claim: LLM answers can change from month to month, making engineering them into complex systems a huge challenge.

quote: Most of the basic technology is well-understood, and to a large degree readily copyable. It's not clear that any commercial company (eg OpenAI) has a durable moat to keep competitors out.
claim: The basic technology behind generative AI is well-understood and readily copyable; no commercial company has a clear durable moat.

quote: The astronomical valuations for Generative AI companies might be justified, but might well not be. Thus far, the valuations seem to be predicated on hopes and dreams, without really factoring in the serious engineering risks.
claim: Generative AI company valuations are predicated on hopes and dreams without factoring in serious engineering risks.

quote: AI is not a magical economic engine; it works brilliantly in some use cases (such as selling ads, helping coders write code faster, playing classic board games) but in many others it simply isn't reliable enough
claim: AI is not a magical economic engine; it works well for some use cases like ads, coding, and games, but is not reliable enough for many others.

quote: Coding is a solid use case that will continue to keep generative AI in the limelight for a long time. Programmers will never go back.
claim: Code assistance is a solid, durable use case for generative AI; programmers will never go back to working without it.

quote: LLMs will probably get cheaper to operate over time, both as the hardware required inevitably gets cheaper, and as people figure out how to make them more efficient.
claim: LLMs will probably get cheaper to operate over time through cheaper hardware and improved efficiency.

quote: things have actually gotten worse than I anticipated.
claim: LLM-driven pollution of the internet has gotten worse than Marcus anticipated.

quote: It's not just the internet, that's been infested; it's Amazon, too, as people are using GPT to create low-quality books
claim: Amazon has been infested with low-quality books generated by GPT.

quote: the search engines are now sucking down each other's garbage
claim: Search engines are ingesting each other's AI-generated errors in a self-reinforcing echo chamber effect.

quote: Cesspools of automatically-generated fake websites, rather than ChatGPT search, may ultimately come to be the single biggest threat that Google ever faces.
claim: Automatically generated fake websites may become the single biggest threat Google has ever faced.

quote: Automatic tools like these will surely accelerate the enshittification of the internet
claim: Automatic AI misinformation tools will accelerate the degradation of internet content quality.

quote: our existing laws are not sufficient to protect us from the full scope of harms that generative AI is beginning to enable
claim: Existing laws are insufficient to protect against the full scope of harms that generative AI enables.

quote: We ought to demand that any content like this that is automatically generated be labeled and watermarked
claim: All automatically generated content should be required to be labeled and watermarked.

quote: we ought to demand that the wholesale generation of misinformation be punishable; there ought to be enforcement
claim: Wholesale generation of misinformation should be punishable by law with enforcement mechanisms.

quote: It highlights the inadequacy of the voluntary agreements such as the one that 7 tech companies recently made with The White House. Those agreements called for watermarking, but because they are voluntary, they do nothing to prevent bad actors from rolling their own automated misinformation software.
claim: Voluntary AI agreements like the White House pact with 7 tech companies are inadequate because they cannot prevent bad actors from creating automated misinformation tools.

quote: An age in which nobody trusts anything may sound like a victory for critical thinking, but it has always been the precise goal of those who wish to flood the zone with shit
claim: Universal mistrust caused by AI-generated misinformation serves the goals of propagandists who aim to flood the zone with disinformation.

quote: What we really need is AI that is smart enough to know when it is being used to spew falsehood. We have no such thing; building it would require a major reshaping of research directions, towards an AI based in facts and competent in reasoning
claim: We need AI capable of detecting when it is being used to generate falsehoods, which would require a major research shift toward fact-based, reasoning-competent AI that does not yet exist.

trigger: California CPUC approves Cruise and Waymo 24/7
quote: Edge cases are everywhere; they are in driving, they are in medicine, they there will be a zillion of them humanoid robots, if and when we roll them out.
claim: Edge cases are pervasive across all domains where AI is applied -- driving, medicine, humanoid robots -- and remain an unsolved problem.

trigger: California CPUC approves Cruise and Waymo 24/7
quote: literally every "self-driving vehicle" on public roads has either a human safety driver on board, or some human somewhere watching remotely who can help the vehicle out when issues arises.
claim: As of 2023, every self-driving vehicle on public roads still requires human supervision, either on board or remote.

trigger: California CPUC approves Cruise and Waymo 24/7
quote: Scaling up to driving everywhere all the time without a well-vetted serious, well-vetted solution to the edge case problem was insane; it was quite literally an accident (or series of accidents) waiting to happen.
claim: Scaling self-driving cars to unrestricted operation without solving the edge case problem is reckless and will inevitably lead to accidents.

trigger: California CPUC approves Cruise and Waymo 24/7
quote: have we discovered the right technology to address edge cases that pervade our messy really world? And if we haven't, shouldn't we stop hammering a square peg into a round hole, and shift our focus towards developing new methodologies for coping with the endless array of edge cases?
claim: Current machine learning technology may not be the right approach for handling real-world edge cases, and research should shift toward developing new methodologies.

trigger: California CPUC approves Cruise and Waymo 24/7
quote: If we don't, we are likely to see reprises of what we see now with driverless cars, in automated doctors, automated psychiatrists, all purpose virtual assistants, home robots, and more, probably for years to come.
claim: Without new methodologies for edge cases, automated doctors, psychiatrists, virtual assistants, and home robots will face the same failures as driverless cars for years to come.

quote: The dirty little secret that pretty much everyone in the AI community knows is that doing well on a benchmark is not actually tantamount to being intelligent.
