quote: The error is not an error of insufficient computational power
claim: Sora's errors in rendering chess boards and similar artifacts are not due to insufficient compute

quote: Nowadays, we have "multimodal" systems, trained on both text and images, and the aforementioned "hallucinations" … and other errors persist and even crop up in new ways.
claim: Adding multimodal grounding (images + text) has not solved LLM confabulation problems

quote: Sora is failing to apprehend a cultural regularity of the world, despite ample evidence
claim: Sora fails to learn cultural regularities like the rules of chess despite ample training data

quote: The system is trying to approximate the world, but it just isn't very good at that job. It uses arrangements of pixels to predict other arrangements of pixels, but it does not try to build an internal model of physics, and it does not try to build an internal model of cultural artifacts, either.
claim: Sora approximates pixel arrangements rather than building internal models of physics or cultural artifacts

quote: In most or all of the errors we have seen thus far, virtually every bit of the images are locally correct; if you just looked at a tiny patch of a given flawed video in isolation, for a single moment, you wouldn't notice anything wrong. What's missing is any global comprehension by the system.
claim: Sora's outputs are locally correct at the pixel level but lack global comprehension of scenes

quote: From AGI perspective, however, this is an immense warning sign. The factuality problems that have haunted LLMs are present even in a new architecture trained on ungodly amounts of data.
claim: Sora's factuality problems despite massive data are a warning sign that new architectures and more data do not solve confabulation for AGI

quote: In my view, these claims — about AGI and world models — are hyperbolic, and unlikely to be true.
claim: Claims that Sora represents progress toward AGI or true world models are hyperbolic and unlikely to be true

quote: OpenAI wants us to believe that this is a "path towards building general purpose simulations of the physical world". As it turns out, that's either hype or confused
claim: OpenAI's claim that Sora is a path to general-purpose physical world simulation is hype or confused

quote: children may be born with object permanence, and certainly have some control it by the age of 4 or 5 months; Sora is never really getting it, even with mountains and mountains of data.
claim: Sora fails at basic object permanence despite massive training data, something human infants achieve by 4-5 months

quote: I predict that many will be hard to remedy. Why? Because the glitches don't stem from the data, they stem from a flaw in how the system reconstructs reality.
claim: Sora's physics glitches will be hard to fix because they stem from fundamental flaws in how the system reconstructs reality, not from data insufficiency

quote: these glitches are in some ways akin to LLM "hallucinations", artifacts from (roughly speaking) decompression from lossy compression. They don't derive from the world.
claim: Sora's physics glitches are analogous to LLM hallucinations -- artifacts of lossy compression rather than reflections of training data

quote: More data won't solve that problem. And like other generative AI systems, there is no way to encode (and guarantee) constraints like "be truthful" or "obey the laws of physics" or "don't just invent (or eliminate) objects".
claim: More data will not solve Sora's physics problems, and there is no way to guarantee constraints like truthfulness or physics compliance in generative AI

quote: Indeed the real lesson here is that Generative AI remains a recalcitrant beast, no matter how much data you throw at it.
claim: Generative AI remains fundamentally unreliable regardless of data scale

quote: Sora is not a solution to AI's longstanding woes with space, time, and causality.
claim: Sora does not solve AI's longstanding problems with space, time, and causality

quote: If a system can't at all handle with the permanence of objects, I am not sure we should even call it a world model at all.
claim: A system that cannot handle object permanence should not be called a world model

quote: Sora is fantastic, but it is akin to morphing and splicing, rather than a path to the physical reasoning we would need for AGI. It is a model of how images change over time, not a model of what entities do in the world.
claim: Sora models how images change over time, not how entities behave in the world, making it irrelevant to AGI

quote: As a solution to artificial general intelligence, though, I see it as a distraction.
claim: Sora is a distraction from, not a path toward, AGI

quote: The need for altogether different technologies that are less opaque, more interpretable, more maintanable, and more debuggable — and hence more tractable—remains paramount.
claim: AI needs fundamentally different technologies that are less opaque, more interpretable, more maintainable, and more debuggable than current LLMs

quote: Generative AI is a kind of alchemy
claim: Generative AI development is a kind of alchemy -- tinkering without deep understanding

quote: these systems have never been been stable. Nobody has ever been able to engineer safety guarantees around then.
claim: Generative AI systems have never been stable and nobody has been able to engineer safety guarantees around them

quote: The AI we have got now isn't really up to the job. Getting the delicate balance here right is way outside current AI's wheelhouse.
claim: Current AI is not capable of balancing diversity representation with historical accuracy in image generation

quote: What one wants is a system that can distinguish past from future, acknowledging history while also playing a role in moving towards a more positive future. And one wants the system to have a nuanced understanding of what is even been asked. (That's the clearly lacking now).
claim: Current AI systems lack the nuanced understanding needed to distinguish historical from contemporary contexts in queries

quote: Some things have scaled exponentially, and some haven't. The capacity to endow machines with the commonsense understanding of history, culture, and human values that would be required to make sensible guardrails has not.
claim: The ability to give AI commonsense understanding of history, culture, and human values has not scaled with compute and data

quote: Maybe, and I am just spitballing, LLMs aren't quite the magic panacea that the industry lead us all to believe?
claim: LLMs are not the transformative panacea the industry has claimed

quote: Sora's can't reliably handle basic physics, ChatGPT had an unexplained meltdown, Gemini can't even remember who was on Apollo 11, there are no formal guarantees that any of this will ever work
claim: There are no formal guarantees that generative AI systems will ever work reliably

quote: The road from research idea to reliable production remains steep. RAG may reduce hallucinations somewhat, but it certainly won't come close to eliminating them.
claim: RAG may reduce LLM hallucinations somewhat but will not come close to eliminating them

quote: To make RAG work reliably would be to solve AGI.
claim: Making RAG work reliably for all queries is as hard as solving AGI

quote: To reliably address all queries, and not just some obvious ones like what was the recent Super Bowl score, would require solutions all the things the field has been struggling to do for 75 years without success.
claim: Reliably answering all queries via RAG would require solving problems AI has failed to solve for 75 years

quote: RAG would need to take natural language and visual input and deconstruct it a logical, computer interpretable form that you can check against databases, and reason over the results. That's really hard.
claim: For RAG to work reliably it would need to decompose natural language into logical forms and reason over them, which is extremely difficult

quote: Imagining perfectly reliable, hallucination-free chatbots by this time next year is bonkers.
claim: Perfectly reliable, hallucination-free chatbots will not exist by early 2025

claim: AI will eventually work reliably, likely requiring neurosymbolic approaches rather than current LLM/RAG methods

quote: Making it work is very likely going to require a whole new technology, one that is unlikely to arrive until the tech industry gets over its infatuation with LLMs and quick fixes.
claim: Reliable AI will require a fundamentally new technology that will not arrive until the industry moves past its LLM fixation

quote: Waiting on RAG to fix what ails LLM is just waiting on another miracle.
claim: RAG is not going to fix the fundamental problems with LLMs

quote: sound advice for the particular circumstance (sternotomy) has been combined with generic advice (about ergonomics) that would sensible in other circumstances but deeply problematic here
claim: GenAI search engines produce dangerous medical errors by combining generically correct advice in contextually inappropriate ways (generative pastiche)

quote: New results from Subbarao Kambhapati cast doubts on the robustness of chain of thought prompting
claim: Chain-of-thought prompting is not robust, based on Kambhapati's research

quote: OpenAI as a whole no longer works according to the mission that was originally set out.

quote: Sora has not learned physics, and is not a reliable guide to how the world works. It is at best a semi-reliable guide to how the world looks.
claim: Sora has not learned physics; it is at best a semi-reliable guide to how the world looks, not how it works

quote: These two—how the world works, and how the world looks—are fundamentally different.
claim: Modeling how the world looks (pixel prediction) is fundamentally different from modeling how the world works (physics)

quote: Sora absolutely fails at object permanence 101. People, animals, limbs, etc routinely wink in and out of existence willy-nilly, as a function of predictions about pixels, rather than as the computations of the states of physical entities.
claim: Sora fails at basic object permanence because it predicts pixels rather than computing states of physical entities

quote: It is precisely because Sora is not a physics engine that a large fraction of Sora's videos are inconsistent with physics or biology, in very basic ways.
claim: A large fraction of Sora's videos are inconsistent with basic physics or biology because Sora is not a physics engine

quote: Whenever there is a conflict between physics and image sequence prediction, image sequence prediction wins. The laws of physics are about objects, not pixels; Sora knows only about pixels and image spaces, not objects.
claim: When physics conflicts with image prediction in Sora, image prediction always wins because Sora operates on pixels, not objects

quote: humanity has now taken a back seat to money; their choices now seem very directly anchored in a need to generate a massive return, in direct contradiction to their promises to California, to Musk, and to the world.
claim: OpenAI now prioritizes profit over its original humanitarian mission, contradicting its promises

trigger: Hofmann et al. covert racism paper

claim: LLMs lack genuine understanding of concepts like people and race; their reliance on superficial statistics drives covert racist stereotyping

trigger: Hofmann et al. covert racism paper
quote: scale actually makes the covert racism worse
claim: Scaling up LLMs makes covert racism worse, not better

trigger: Hofmann et al. covert racism paper
quote: I honestly don't see an easy fix — simply adding human feedback made things worse, as the authors showed. And Google's debacle shows that guardrails are never easy.
claim: There is no easy fix for covert racism in LLMs; RLHF makes it worse and guardrails are unreliable

trigger: Hofmann et al. covert racism paper

claim: LLM companies should recall their systems until covert racism is adequately addressed

claim: Deep learning will turn out to be only a small part of what is needed for trustworthy AI

claim: Deep learning is best suited for low-stakes tasks where approximate results are acceptable

trigger: 2-year anniversary of Marcus's 'Deep Learning Is Hitting a Wall' paper

claim: Current deep learning systems frequently make basic errors

trigger: 2-year anniversary of Marcus's 'Deep Learning Is Hitting a Wall' paper
quote: scaling laws aren't universal laws like gravity but rather mere observations that might not hold forever, much like Moore's law
claim: AI scaling laws are empirical observations that may not hold indefinitely, analogous to Moore's law

trigger: 2-year anniversary of Marcus's 'Deep Learning Is Hitting a Wall' paper

claim: LLMs alone are not the answer to AGI; a paradigm shift is needed

trigger: 2-year anniversary of Marcus's 'Deep Learning Is Hitting a Wall' paper

claim: The metrics that have improved with scaling do not capture genuine comprehension

trigger: 2-year anniversary of Marcus's 'Deep Learning Is Hitting a Wall' paper
quote: Toxic language can easily be elicited by jailbreaks, but less so in ordinary use; misinformation is still common, even without jailbreaks
claim: LLM toxicity has improved in normal use but remains elicitable via jailbreaks; misinformation remains common even without jailbreaks

claim: Even prominent AI leaders (Gates, Hassabis, LeCun, Altman) have recognized that AI scaling may be hitting a plateau

quote: why $7T, if the ROI isn't yet clear?
claim: The ROI on generative AI investment is not yet clear despite massive spending

trigger: Retracted rat paper with AI-generated images
quote: GenAI is messing with science. And it may get a lot worse.
claim: Generative AI is degrading scientific integrity and the problem may worsen significantly

trigger: Retracted rat paper with AI-generated images
quote: the long term consequences on science may be severe

trigger: Retracted rat paper with AI-generated images

claim: GenAI-generated garbage submissions could overwhelm journal review processes, damaging scientific publishing reputation

trigger: Mira Murati WSJ interview on Sora training data
quote: Aside from all that, Murati claimed she had no idea what specific data Sora was trained on; given her role as CTO that seems all but inconceivable.
claim: OpenAI CTO Mira Murati's claim of not knowing what data Sora was trained on is not credible given her role

trigger: Mira Murati WSJ interview on Sora training data
quote: Half truths about openness, about what data they are training on, about finances, about their views on regulation, about when if ever they would pause, and more, essentially every topic that matters, from the day they were launched all the way to the present. A lack of full candor seems to run straight through the corporate DNA.
claim: A pattern of half-truths and lack of candor on openness, data, finances, and regulation runs through OpenAI's corporate DNA

trigger: Discovery of ChatGPT remnant phrases in scientific papers
quote: every article with ChatGPT remnants should be considered suspect and perhaps retracted, because hallucinations may have filtered in, and both authors and reviewers) were asleep at the switch.
claim: Every scientific article containing ChatGPT remnant phrases should be considered suspect and possibly retracted because hallucinations may have infiltrated the content

trigger: Discovery of ChatGPT remnant phrases in scientific papers
quote: Reviewers are typically unpaid academics who are already stretched to their limits; tripling their workload would not be feasible. And GenAI might do a lot worse than merely tripling workloads; the total number of articles may radically spike, many of them dubious and a waste of reviewers' time.
claim: GenAI will cause a radical spike in dubious scientific paper submissions that overwhelms the already-strained peer review system

trigger: Discovery of ChatGPT remnant phrases in scientific papers
quote: If science journals and science itself are overrun by LLM-generated garbage, as now seems imminent, my agnostic "I can't quite tell yet if LLMs will ultimately be of net benefit to society" is going to switch to "shut it down if they can't fix this problem."
claim: Science journals being overrun by LLM-generated garbage is imminent and may tip the cost-benefit analysis of LLMs to net negative for society

trigger: Discovery of ChatGPT remnant phrases in scientific papers
quote: Literally the only other solution I can envision is to force LLM manufacturers to pay a significant tax to subsidize the journal review system that they are about to destroy.
claim: LLM manufacturers should be taxed to subsidize the journal review system that their products are degrading

quote: We might be reaching a plateau in terms of sheer capability. Nobody has been able to beat it decisively.
claim: AI capabilities may be reaching a plateau at the GPT-4 level, with no competitor decisively surpassing it after one year.

quote: Places like Google and Anthropic have put in a lot of money trying. None succeeded; instead there tentatively seems to be convergence at GPT-4 levels.
claim: Google and Anthropic have spent heavily but converged at GPT-4-level performance rather than surpassing it.

quote: putting LLMs into production is hard; most work to date has been preliminary. Companies are starting to temper their expectations. Many initial expectations were unrealistic.
claim: Most enterprise LLM deployments have been preliminary rather than production-level, and initial corporate expectations for LLMs were unrealistic.

quote: Bad actors, who may have lower standards for reliability, appear to be using them for cybercrime and disinformation.
claim: Bad actors are using LLMs for cybercrime and disinformation because they have lower reliability standards than legitimate users.

quote: Some core problems have lingered, particularly hallucinations, instability and occasional nonsensical errors.
claim: Core LLM problems including hallucinations, instability, and nonsensical errors remain unsolved after one year of GPT-4.

quote: OpenAI was here first, but there's not a huge moat; many other players appear to be catching up. We can expect price wars.
claim: OpenAI has no significant competitive moat in LLMs, and price wars among LLM providers are expected.

quote: Profitability remains in question. Big models cost big $, and getting standout results might cost huge $; licensing and legal costs may be substantial.
claim: The profitability of large language models remains questionable due to high training, operating, licensing, and legal costs.

trigger: OpenAI VP Anna Makanju remarks at Columbia
quote: the inherent unreliability in these systems is deeply worrisome
claim: The inherent unreliability of LLM-based AI systems is a serious concern that undermines their positive applications.

quote: If lots of code gets written, fast, but that code is riddled with security problems, the net advantage on the positive side of the ledger may be less than anticipated.
claim: AI-generated code may produce a net negative due to security vulnerabilities, offsetting productivity gains.

quote: I am increasingly worried that the big tech companies are—in conjunction with a Congress that has not yet passed any substantive AI legislation—successfully architecting a grossly asymmetric world, in which the profits from the positive side flow to them, and the costs of the negative side are borne by everyone else.
claim: Big tech companies are structuring the AI ecosystem so that they capture the profits while society bears the costs of AI harms.

quote: If the 2024 election flips based on GenAI-created disinformation, there will be a huge cost to society, but it is unlikely that the GenAI companies will be held responsible in any serious way.
claim: If GenAI-created disinformation influences the 2024 election, AI companies are unlikely to be held legally responsible.

quote: poorly-written software, authored in part by GenAI, could spread malware leading to a massive security incident that dwarfs WannaCry
claim: GenAI-assisted code could lead to a massive security incident worse than WannaCry due to poor code quality.

quote: A $100B LLM is still an LLM. Driverless cars were crippled by the endless long tail of outliers; LLMs may well pattern similarly, working in many cases but often troubled by the unexpected, even when scaled.
claim: A $100B LLM will still suffer from the same fundamental limitations as current LLMs, particularly failure on outlier cases, regardless of scale.

quote: Reasoning and planning in unexpected scenarios are still likely to be flawed.
claim: LLM reasoning and planning in unexpected scenarios will remain flawed even at $100B scale.

quote: Even if a $100B LLM worked noticeably better than a $1B LLM, the economics for that investment are problematic.
claim: The economics of a $100B LLM are problematic because diminishing returns mean it would only be marginally better than far cheaper models on most tasks.

quote: Presumably the operating idea here is that (a) $100B will equal AGI and (b) that customers will pay literally anything for AGI. I doubt either premise is correct.
claim: Spending $100B on an LLM will not produce AGI, and customers would not pay unlimited fees for AGI even if it existed.

quote: If they are wrong, the net effect will be to delay AGI, because their project will siphon away talent from other approaches – just as it already has, but more so.
claim: The $100B Stargate project will delay AGI by siphoning talent away from alternative approaches to AI.

trigger: Wall Street Journal report on GenAI spending vs revenue
quote: $50B in, $3B out. That's obviously not sustainable.
claim: The generative AI industry spending $50B while generating only $3B in revenue is not a sustainable business model.

quote: If hallucinations were brought down to human expert levels by the end of 2024 I would be truly astonished. They are inherent to how the system makes predictions, and to the fact that LLMs themselves don't have the wherewithal to look things up and reason over them.
claim: LLM hallucinations will not be reduced to human expert levels by end of 2024 because they are inherent to how LLMs make predictions.

quote: The entire industry is based on hype, and on the specific hope that the kinds of problems we saw again and again with GPT-2, GPT-3, and GPT-4, not to mention Claude, Grok, Gemini, LLaMA, and so on, are on the verge of being solved — even as the reality is that all the big players are converging on systems that are roughly equivalent to GPT-4, all flawed in essentially the same ways.
claim: The GenAI industry is based on hype rather than substance, with all major models converging at GPT-4-level performance and sharing the same fundamental flaws.

quote: I am not confident Generative AI as currently envisioned will ever come together.
claim: Generative AI as currently envisioned may never achieve the reliability and capability needed to justify its costs.

quote: With everyone building similar models, there is no moat, hence lots of competition on price
claim: There is no competitive moat in LLMs because all major companies are building similar models, leading to price competition.

quote: current-sized models, already expensive, aren't reliable enough for many jobs
claim: Current LLMs are too unreliable for many production use cases while already being expensive to operate.

quote: So far most uses of LLMs have been for testing, not production-level systems.
claim: Most enterprise LLM deployments as of early 2024 are testing/experimental rather than production systems.

quote: the closest thing to a killer app is probably coding assistance, but the income there isn't massive enough to cover costs of the chips, legal etc.
claim: Coding assistance is the closest thing to a killer app for LLMs, but it does not generate enough revenue to cover infrastructure and legal costs.

quote: The initially-celebrated use case of "generative search" isn't reliable enough (and doesn't yet have a clear business model).
claim: Generative AI-powered search is not reliable enough for production use and lacks a viable business model.

quote: GPT-5 may be more satisfying but is likely even more expensive to train and operate.
claim: GPT-5 will likely be even more expensive to train and operate than GPT-4, compounding the profitability problem.

quote: If nobody (OpenAI, Google, or anyone else) releases a true quantum leap by the end of 2024, substantially addressing key issues around reliability, hallucination, data leakage, and security, the bubble may start to pop by this time next year.
claim: If no company releases a transformative AI model addressing reliability, hallucination, and security by end of 2024, the GenAI bubble may start popping by early 2025.

quote: GenAI will still exist, and find some use, but valuations and excitement may dissipate, reemerging only once genuine advances are made.
claim: Generative AI will persist but at reduced valuations and excitement levels until genuine technical advances are made.

quote: Nobody knows exactly how LLMs work, and that means that nobody can ever issue strong guarantees around them.
claim: It is impossible to provide strong security guarantees for LLMs because nobody fully understands how they work internally.

quote: Rule 1 of cybersecurity is to keep your attack surface small; in LLMs the attack surface appears to be infinite. That can't be good.
claim: LLMs have an effectively infinite attack surface, making them fundamentally insecure.

quote: Secure, trustworthy AI could be a great thing, but LLMs will never be that.
claim: LLMs will never be secure and trustworthy AI systems.

quote: If we don't get a handle on this problem, fast, nobody will believe anything.
claim: Without swift action against AI-generated misinformation, public trust in all information will collapse.

quote: fast, cheap, automated misinformation left unchecked threatens to undermine trust — and democracy itself.
claim: Unchecked AI-generated misinformation threatens to undermine both public trust and democratic institutions.

quote: we should require all AI-generated content to be labeled
claim: All AI-generated content should be required by law to be labeled as such.

trigger: New preprint on data and scaling (arXiv:2404.04125)
quote: Neural networks (at least in the configurations that have been dominant over the last three decades) have trouble generalizing beyond the multidimensional space that surrounds their training examples.
claim: Neural networks in their dominant configurations cannot reliably generalize beyond the space of their training examples.

trigger: New preprint on data and scaling (arXiv:2404.04125)
quote: That limits their ability to reason and plan reliably. It also drives their greediness with data
claim: Neural networks' inability to generalize beyond training data limits their reasoning and planning ability and drives their insatiable need for more data.

trigger: New preprint on data and scaling (arXiv:2404.04125)
quote: This is why driverless cars are still just demos, and why LLMs will never be reliable.
claim: LLMs will never be reliable because neural networks cannot generalize beyond their training distribution.

trigger: New preprint on data and scaling (arXiv:2404.04125)
quote: Sooner or later, the exponential greed for data will exceed what is available.
claim: The exponentially growing data requirements of LLMs will eventually exceed available training data.

trigger: New preprint on data and scaling (arXiv:2404.04125)
quote: To get to AGI, we need alternative approaches that can generalize better beyond the data on which they have been trained.
claim: Achieving AGI requires fundamentally different approaches from current neural networks that can generalize beyond training data.

trigger: NYT report on AI companies cutting ethical corners for data
quote: the brittleness (a term for unreliability); the greed for data that is causing the desperation that the Times described; the opacity (uninterpretability) that makes debugging, fairness, and engineering so hard; and the shallowness, meaning that generalizations are never complete.
claim: The four fundamental problems of deep learning identified in 2018 -- brittleness, data greed, opacity, and shallowness -- are exactly what is ailing GenAI in 2024.

trigger: NYT report on AI companies cutting ethical corners for data
quote: an intellectual monoculture — with no alternative approach that is nearly as well-funded
claim: AI research has become an intellectual monoculture focused on LLMs, with no alternative approach receiving comparable funding.

trigger: NYT report on AI companies cutting ethical corners for data
quote: a pile of unreliable systems that are unlikely to live up to the hype
claim: Current generative AI systems are unreliable and unlikely to live up to the hype surrounding them.

trigger: NYT report on AI companies cutting ethical corners for data
quote: We must invest heavily in alternative approaches and stop funding a losing hand to the infinite degree, crowding everything out.
claim: The AI field should invest heavily in alternative approaches to LLMs rather than continuing to pour resources into scaling current architectures.

trigger: Elon Musk AGI prediction on X Space
quote: I don't buy the predictions for the next year
claim: Musk's prediction that AI will be smarter than any individual human by end of 2025 is wrong.

quote: some late stage venture capital firms are sitting out the current GenAI rush
claim: Late-stage venture capital firms are beginning to avoid investing in the GenAI rush, signaling the bubble may not last.

quote: I could be persuaded that on some measures there was a doubling of capabilities for some set of months in 2020-2023, but I don't see that case at all for the last 13 months.
claim: LLM capabilities have not doubled in the 13 months since GPT-4's release, contrary to claims of continued exponential improvement.

quote: Instead, I see numerous signs that we have reached a period of diminishing returns.
claim: LLM development has entered a period of diminishing returns as of early 2024.

quote: Mollick's claim that "the rate of improvement in Large Language models show capabilities doubling every 5 to 14 months" is no longer holding true.
claim: The claim that LLM capabilities double every 5-14 months is no longer supported by recent evidence.

quote: The wall that I once warned about, in 2022, may finally be approaching.
claim: LLMs are approaching a capability wall or ceiling that limits further improvement.

quote: If we really have changed regimes, from rapid progress to diminishing returns, and hallucinations and stupid errors do linger, LLMs may never be ready for prime time.
claim: If LLM progress has shifted to diminishing returns, LLMs may never be reliable enough for production use.

quote: In the most extreme case, OpenAI's $86 billion valuation could look in hindsight like a WeWork moment for AI.
claim: OpenAI's $86 billion valuation may prove to be as overinflated as WeWork's was.

quote: If enthusiasm for GenAI dwindles and market valuations plummet, AI won't disappear, and LLMs won't disappear; they will still have their place as tools for statistical approximation.
claim: Even if the GenAI bubble bursts, LLMs will persist as useful tools for statistical approximation but at a lower scale than currently envisioned.

quote: Reliable, trustworthy AI is surely achievable, but we may need to go back to the drawing board to get there.
claim: Achieving reliable and trustworthy AI will require fundamentally new approaches beyond current LLM architectures.

quote: there about 5 - 7 recent models are on a par with GPT-4, but none is clearly and decisively ahead
claim: As of April 2024, 5-7 recent LLMs match GPT-4's level but none decisively surpasses it, suggesting convergence.

quote: Both damning and correct. People who believe in LLMs have played themselves.
claim: People who attribute deep intelligence to LLMs are being deceived by a mechanism analogous to a psychic's con -- the LLMentalist Effect.

quote: the cognitive capacities of LLMs are severely limited
claim: The cognitive capacities of LLMs are severely limited despite their impressive surface-level fluency.

quote: Right now there are likely hundreds, maybe thousands or even millions of such things.
claim: There are hundreds to millions of intellectual tasks that ordinary humans can do that AI cannot as of 2024.

quote: Reliably drive a car in a novel location that they haven't previously encountered, even in the face of unusual circumstances like hand-lettered signs, without the assistance of other humans.
claim: AI will not be able to reliably drive a car in novel locations with unusual circumstances by end of 2025.

quote: Learn and master the basics of almost any new video game within a few minutes or hours, and solve original puzzles in the alternate world of that video game.
claim: AI will not be able to master arbitrary new video games within minutes or hours by end of 2025.

quote: Write engaging biographies and obituaries without obvious hallucinations that aren't grounded in reliable sources.
claim: AI will not be able to write biographies and obituaries without hallucinations by end of 2025.

quote: Write cogent, persuasive legal briefs without hallucinating any cases
claim: AI will not be able to write legal briefs without hallucinating case citations by end of 2025.

quote: Write Pulitzer-caliber books, fiction and non-fiction
claim: AI will not be able to write Pulitzer-caliber books by end of 2025.

quote: Come up with paradigm-shifting, Nobel-caliber scientific discoveries.
claim: AI will not produce Nobel-caliber paradigm-shifting scientific discoveries by end of 2025.

quote: All of the above seem outside the bounds of current AI. None seem to me to be within immediate reach.
claim: A wide range of tasks from physical navigation to creative writing to scientific discovery remain outside the reach of current and near-term AI.

quote: they will not themselves, anytime soon, be virtual scientists
claim: LLMs will not function as virtual scientists capable of independent scientific discovery anytime soon.

quote: the multimodal stuff still is very buggy, having troubles with basics like the English alphabet
claim: Multimodal AI capabilities remain very buggy, struggling with basic tasks like rendering the English alphabet correctly.

quote: Yejin Choi worried that LLMs were not reliable. They still aren't. She was right.
claim: LLMs remain unreliable as of April 2024, confirming warnings from researchers like Yejin Choi.

quote: I warned that LLMs would be used by bad actors, and they increasingly are.
claim: LLMs are increasingly being used by bad actors for harmful purposes as predicted.

quote: hallucinations (e.g., on basic math) were again the key liability. You can't teach if your students don't know whether or not to believe you.
claim: LLM hallucinations are a fundamental barrier to using AI for education because students cannot trust unreliable outputs.

quote: I continue to have doubts about whether LLMs are really the right tool for the next step of the job
claim: LLMs may not be the right technology for advancing educational tools like Khan Academy.

quote: no company yet had A.I. technology that was sophisticated enough to make a virtual assistant answer questions reliably
claim: No company as of April 2024 has AI technology sophisticated enough to make a virtual assistant reliably answer questions.

quote: It's almost like a broken watch being right twice a day. It's right some of the time, but you don't know which part of the time, and that greatly diminishes its value.
claim: AI virtual assistants are unreliable in a way that greatly diminishes their value -- users cannot predict when they will be correct.

quote: I don't think we are any closer to a machine with the wherewithal or the motivation to annihilate the human species than we were a year ago.
claim: AI has not moved meaningfully closer to posing an existential risk to humanity between 2023 and 2024.

quote: Bugs in LLMs have stymied Khanmigo and the AI pin. Adding visual data to LLMs hasn't solved hallucinations.
claim: Adding multimodal capabilities to LLMs has not solved the hallucination problem.

trigger: TED 2024 talks by Suleyman, Khosla, Li
quote: I don't see how we get to a positive AI future if we sweep all the problems under the rug.
claim: Achieving a positive AI future requires honestly confronting current AI problems rather than ignoring them.

trigger: TED 2024 talks by Suleyman, Khosla, Li
quote: I think Khosla's timing (all this by 2049) might be off, perhaps by decades
claim: Vinod Khosla's predictions about AI transforming medicine, agriculture, and infrastructure by 2049 may be off by decades.

trigger: TED 2024 talks by Suleyman, Khosla, Li
quote: current AI needs better spatial intelligence
claim: Current AI systems need fundamentally better spatial intelligence to achieve real-world physical capabilities.

trigger: TED 2024 talks by Suleyman, Khosla, Li
quote: We won't get to a billion personal robots if they are as dodgy as driverless cars, frequently working, yet stymied often enough by outliers that we can't fully count on them.
claim: Achieving widespread personal robotics requires solving the reliability problem that plagues driverless cars -- robots that fail on outlier cases cannot be trusted.

trigger: TED 2024 talks by Suleyman, Khosla, Li
quote: A billion robots with a superficial understanding of the physical world would be a recipe for disaster.
claim: Deploying robots at scale with only superficial physical world understanding would be dangerous.

trigger: TED 2024 talks by Suleyman, Khosla, Li
quote: Dreamers who promise far more than they are likely to deliver are getting almost all the money, and (at least in AI) they are getting so much money for a path that it is unlikely to work that they are leaving too little oxygen for the exploration of new ideas that might work.
claim: Venture capital is disproportionately funding AI approaches that are unlikely to work, starving alternative approaches of resources.

trigger: TED 2024 talks by Suleyman, Khosla, Li
quote: I doubt that anyone with a short leash can achieve the breakthroughs we will need, if we are to get to the optimistic future so many of us would like to see.
claim: Achieving AI breakthroughs requires long-term research funding, not the typical 3-year VC timeline for product-market fit.

trigger: TED 2024 talks by Suleyman, Khosla, Li
quote: Cars would never have blossomed without streetlights and traffic laws, and too many people would have died without seatbelts.
claim: AI regulation is necessary for AI to flourish, just as traffic laws were necessary for automobiles to succeed.

trigger: TED 2024 talks by Suleyman, Khosla, Li
quote: We don't want AI to wind up stigmatized like nuclear energy, a good solution that was rolled out with insufficient care and permanently tarnished.
claim: Without proper regulation and care, AI risks becoming permanently stigmatized like nuclear energy.

quote: Like so many recent models, the new model approached GPT-4 but didn't exceed it, consistent (but not proof of) my ongoing conjecture of diminishing returns.
claim: Meta's LLAMA-3 approached but did not exceed GPT-4, providing further evidence for the hypothesis of diminishing returns in LLM scaling.

quote: Whatever tiny moat there was between the companies will further diminish. LLMs are well on their way to becoming a commodity; price wars will be intense.
claim: LLMs are becoming commoditized, which will erode competitive advantages and trigger intense price wars among providers.

trigger: Meta LLAMA-3 release and stock market decline
quote: 2024 could be remembered as the high point in a kind of tulip-mania.
claim: 2024 may mark the peak of a tulip-mania-like bubble in AI investment.

quote: all the major AI companies are spending billions producing almost exactly the same results using almost exactly the same data using almost exactly the same technology — all flawed in almost exactly the same ways
claim: All major AI companies are producing nearly identical LLM results with the same technology and data, all sharing the same fundamental flaws -- a dangerous groupthink.

quote: hallucinations are absolutely core to how LLMs work, and that we need new approaches based on different ideas.
claim: Hallucinations are a fundamental feature of how LLMs work, not a fixable bug, and solving the problem requires fundamentally new approaches.

quote: LLM "hallucinations" arise, regularly, because (a) they literally don't know the difference between truth and falsehood, (b) they don't have reliably reasoning processes to guarantee that their inferences are correct and (c) they are incapable of fact-checking their own work
claim: LLM hallucinations arise because LLMs cannot distinguish truth from falsehood, lack reliable reasoning processes, and cannot fact-check their own output

quote: everything that LLMs say -- true or false -- comes from the same process of statistically reconstructing what words are likely in some context. They NEVER fact-check what they say.
claim: All LLM outputs, whether true or false, come from the same statistical word-prediction process with no internal fact-checking mechanism

quote: even with perfect data, the stochastic reconstructive process would still produce some errors. The very process that LLMs use to generalize also creates hallucinations.
claim: Even with perfect training data, the stochastic generalization process used by LLMs would still produce hallucinations because the same mechanism that enables generalization also produces errors

quote: The field of AI will eventually do better, but probably with an AI that is structured differently, in which facts are first-class citizens, rather than something you hope you might get for free with enough data.
claim: Future AI systems that surpass current LLM limitations will likely need a different architecture where facts are first-class citizens rather than emergent from statistical patterns

claim: There is a real possibility that 2025 could see the worst cyberattack in history, potentially catalyzed by generative AI

quote: Depressingly, despite all of the apparent progress in the last few years, much of what we raised in 2015 and re-emphasized in 2019 remains unsolved.
claim: The core problems of commonsense reasoning in AI identified in 2015 and 2019 remain unsolved despite recent progress in AI

quote: no single technique would be adequate in itself, and that the solution to the problem would probably require new techniques, as well as better integration between those that already existed
claim: Solving commonsense reasoning will require new techniques and better integration of existing approaches, not any single technique alone

quote: Rosie the Robot is not doing our housework, and that is in part because the kind of physical reasoning a household robot would need to do can't be done purely by running simulations
claim: Physical simulation alone cannot achieve the commonsense physical reasoning required for general-purpose household robots

quote: In Elon Musk's recent Optimus demonstrations, all or most of the commonsense required was apparently outsourced to human teleoperators.
claim: Tesla's Optimus robot demonstrations relied on human teleoperators for commonsense reasoning rather than autonomous AI

quote: more recent models no longer makes the same specific mistakes that GPT-3 did, but still lack a robust understanding of the real external world
claim: Recent LLMs have fixed specific mistakes of earlier models but still lack robust understanding of the external physical world

quote: because it does not understand how objects are embedded in space and interact in space or, indeed, what spatial relationships actually mean, it often makes very basic mistakes
claim: OpenAI's o1 does not understand how objects are embedded in and interact in space, leading to basic spatial reasoning mistakes

quote: There is not much reason to think that a robot could actually use such video streams in a practical sense to reliably navigate, act, and plan in the real world.
claim: Video generation systems like Sora cannot produce outputs that robots could use for reliable real-world navigation, action, and planning

quote: fundamentally its videos are more like visual collages than the renderings of game engines, which is why they sometimes have a surreal or magic trick quality
claim: Sora's videos are visual collages rather than physics-based renderings, which is why they exhibit surreal physical behavior

quote: they will not work well until major progress is made in common sense, which itself may require wholesale revisions in how AI is approached
claim: AI agents will not work well until major progress is made on commonsense reasoning, which may require wholesale revisions in AI approaches

quote: Hoping that reliable commonsense will simply "emerge" from large databases of text and video has proven unrealistic, again and again.
claim: Reliable commonsense reasoning will not emerge from simply training on large databases of text and video

quote: one important component of physical and conceptual understanding is reasoning about entities and their properties, and current approaches have consistently fallen short on this
claim: Current AI approaches consistently fall short on reasoning about entities and their properties, a key component of physical and conceptual understanding

quote: it is only once AI researchers grapple directly with the challenging problems inherent in commonsense reasoning about entities that interact in space and persist in time that the field of general-purpose AI will finally begin to mature
claim: General-purpose AI will only mature once researchers directly tackle commonsense reasoning about entities that interact in space and persist in time

quote: the best was Claude 3.5, at 24%

claim: LLMs generalize well to similar items but struggle with reliability in unfamiliar regimes, with even minor variations sometimes breaking them

trigger: Sam Altman blog post claiming AGI confidence

trigger: Sam Altman blog post claiming AGI confidence

claim: Pure LLM scaling has reached a period of diminishing returns, as Marcus predicted in 2022

trigger: Sam Altman blog post claiming AGI confidence
quote: the lack of distinct, accessible, reliable database-style records leads to hallucinations
claim: The lack of distinct, accessible, reliable database-style records in LLMs is a root cause of hallucinations

quote: the definition of AGI, while not perfect, is reasonably clear — and has been reasonably clear — for a long time
claim: The definition of AGI has been reasonably clear for a long time

quote: a shorthand for any intelligence ... that is flexible and general, with resourcefulness and reliability comparable to (or beyond) human intelligence
claim: AGI should be defined as intelligence that is flexible, general, resourceful, and reliable, comparable to or beyond human intelligence

quote: One could imagine, for example, replacing every single job one job at a time, each with its own separate domain-specific systems. That would be impressive, but would not reflect the General in Artificial General Intelligence.
claim: Replacing jobs one at a time with domain-specific systems would not constitute AGI even if economically significant

quote: Current models trained on next-token prediction have remarkable abilities and remarkable weaknesses. The scope of those abilities and weaknesses of the current systems is not well understood.
claim: Current next-token-prediction models have remarkable abilities and remarkable weaknesses whose scope is not well understood

quote: there is zero justification for claiming that the current technology has achieved general intelligence
claim: There is no justification for claiming current AI technology has achieved general intelligence

quote: it's becoming increasingly clear that they are struggling in building a model worthy of the GPT-5 name
claim: OpenAI is struggling to build a model worthy of the GPT-5 name

quote: We might see GPT-5 this year, but given bottlenecks on data and inherent limits in LLMs we may well not.
claim: GPT-5 may not ship in 2025 due to data bottlenecks and inherent LLM limitations

quote: The hypothesis I have been floating since 2022 that scaling data alone would not be enough for robust AI has become increasingly prevalent
claim: Scaling data alone is not sufficient for achieving robust AI

quote: 500,000 humanoid robots in three years, which seems to very unlikely
claim: Elon Musk's prediction of 500,000 humanoid robots in three years is very unlikely to be achieved

quote: I will stand by my own predictions that we won't see AGI in 2025
claim: AGI will not arrive in 2025

quote: I won't be at all surprised if don't even see anything worthy of the GPT-5 name
claim: It would not be surprising if nothing worthy of the GPT-5 name ships in 2025

quote: LLMs don't meet that; as the world has discovered, reliability is not their strong suit
claim: LLMs do not meet the definition of AGI because reliability is not their strong suit

quote: LLMs lack the ability to reliably generalize to novel circumstances
claim: LLMs lack the ability to reliably generalize to novel circumstances

quote: the inability of LLMs to do basic fact checking and sanity checking speak to their lack of resourcefulness
claim: LLMs are unable to perform basic fact-checking and sanity-checking of their own outputs

quote: Counting on AI that is shallow and not reliable, it should also be said, could cause huge problems. We can never guarantee that broad but shallow AI will be safe, or that it will be human-compatible
claim: Relying on broad but shallow AI that is unreliable could cause huge problems because its safety and human-compatibility cannot be guaranteed

quote: LLMs often use the words without a deep understanding what those words mean
claim: LLMs use words without deep understanding of what those words mean

quote: A system answering a river-crossing problem should not answer by superficial analogy to the words in similar problems found in a giant scrape of the internet, but rather by reasoning through concepts about boats, rivers, people, and so on.
claim: AI systems should solve problems by reasoning through concepts rather than by superficial word-level analogy to training data

quote: Moving past broad but shallow AI and into a new and hopefully safer and more reliable regime should be a major research priority for the human species.
claim: Moving past broad-but-shallow AI toward safer and more reliable AI should be a major research priority

quote: GenAI answers are frequently superficial; they invent things ("hallucinations" or what I would prefer to call confabulations"), they fail to sanity check their own work, and they regularly make boneheaded errors in reasoning, mathematics and so on.
claim: Generative AI answers are frequently superficial, produce hallucinations, fail to self-check, and regularly make errors in reasoning and mathematics

quote: I do genuinely think we will all have our own AI agents, and companies will have armies of them. And they will be worth trillions, since eventually (no time soon) they will do a huge fraction of all human knowledge work, and maybe physical labor too.
claim: AI agents will eventually do a huge fraction of all human knowledge work and possibly physical labor, and will be worth trillions

quote: But not this year (or next, or the one after that, and probably not this decade, except in narrow use cases). All that we will have this year are demos.
claim: Reliable general-purpose AI agents will not arrive in 2025, 2026, 2027, or probably this decade, except in narrow use cases

quote: And those demos won't work all that reliably.
claim: AI agent demos in 2025 will not work reliably

quote: you can't build reliable agents unless you can solve the hallucination problem, and there is still no principled trustworthy answer to that problem
claim: Reliable AI agents cannot be built until the hallucination problem is solved, and no principled solution to hallucination exists yet

quote: you can't build agents unless you have common sense, sound reasoning, a tight grounding in reality, and systems that reliably do you what you ask of them, with enough theory of mind to infer your intentions
claim: Reliable AI agents require commonsense, sound reasoning, grounding in reality, reliable instruction-following, and theory of mind

quote: Easily jailbroken AI agents that excel at mimicry but lack the capacity and imperative to evaluate the consequences of their actions are going to cause massive harm.
claim: Easily jailbroken AI agents that lack the ability to evaluate consequences of their actions will cause massive harm

quote: "Mainlining them" into the veins of society, as the UK proposes to do, is a huge mistake.
claim: The UK's proposal to deeply integrate AI agents into society is a huge mistake given their security vulnerabilities

trigger: Sam Altman walking back AGI claims
quote: stands by his prediction that we will not see AGI in 2025
claim: AGI will not be achieved in 2025

claim: AI infrastructure spending (~$250B industry-wide) has enormously outweighed total AI revenue, perhaps by a ratio of 50:1

quote: The entirely speculative conjecture that any profits will actually do much to help the American people, as opposed to just enriching those who own that infrastructure.
claim: It is speculative that AI infrastructure profits will help the American people broadly rather than just enriching infrastructure owners

claim: If AI data centers produce better AI, the net employment effect is entirely unclear because job losses may offset jobs created

quote: Advances will be more incremental than before and quickly matched. GPT-5 or a similarly impressive model will come eventually
claim: Future AI advances will be more incremental than before and will be quickly matched by competitors

quote: Models will continue to get more efficient and less expensive, but hallucinations and reliability problems will persist.

claim: Unregulated LLM competition combined with unreliable AI will exacerbate risks of misinformation, bias, privacy breaches, and misuse

quote: Nvidia could soon take a serious hit

quote: It doesn't solve hallucinations or problems with reliability.
claim: DeepSeek R1 does not solve the hallucination or reliability problems of LLMs

quote: It is still expensive to operate ("inference"), especially if you want to make it "think" longer like o3.
claim: DeepSeek R1 is still expensive to operate at inference time, especially with extended reasoning like o3

quote: The biggest threat isn't to Nvidia (people will still need GPUs, albeit fewer higher end ones), but rather to OpenAI and Anthropic (because price wars will undercut their hopes of making a profit).
claim: DeepSeek's biggest threat is to OpenAI and Anthropic's profitability via price wars, not to Nvidia's business

quote: DeepSeek is an economic revolution, and geopolitical wake-up call, but that doesn't directly bring us any closer to AGI.

claim: It is not clear that building human-level AI actually requires millions of chips, since humans achieve flexible general intelligence with only ~20 watts

quote: The real lesson of DeepSeek is that we can still make vast improvements in efficency. When all is said and done, with the right software, we might be able to build intelligence with hundreds or even dozens of GPUs.
claim: With the right software, it may be possible to build AI intelligence using only hundreds or dozens of GPUs rather than millions

quote: 2026-2027 are made up dates. I hereby offer Dario a $100,000 bet that we will not have AI that is 'smarter than almost all human beings' by the end of 2027
claim: AI will not be smarter than almost all human beings by the end of 2027

quote: so far those controls have not been terribly successful; and may even have had paradoxical effects
claim: US AI chip export controls have not been successful and may have had paradoxical effects by spurring Chinese innovation

quote: the LLM race is an enormously expensive effort to win a race that can no longer be won

claim: The country that develops new AI technology that is more reliable and harder to replicate than LLMs will win the AI race

quote: what we really need are better ideas, and the oxygen for innovative startups to breathe
claim: Winning the AI race requires better ideas and support for innovative startups, not just more chips for incumbents

quote: a company that made its name regurgitating and recombining sliced-up bits of intellectual property in statistically probable ways without due compensation is now threatened by … another company apparently doing the same, at lower cost
claim: OpenAI built its business by using others' intellectual property without due compensation and is now being threatened by DeepSeek doing the same to them

quote: Deep Research, because it works faster and expands the reach of what can be automatically generated, is going to make that problem worse.
claim: OpenAI's Deep Research will worsen the problem of AI-generated misinformation and garbage content on the internet

claim: Scammers will use Deep Research to generate half-baked medical reports for ad-driven websites

quote: the larger problem may be naive people who believe that the outputs are legit, or who use it as a shortcut for writing journal articles, missing the errors it produces
claim: The larger problem with Deep Research is naive users who will believe its outputs are legitimate or use it as a shortcut for journal articles while missing its errors

quote: virtually everything that Deep Research produces will pass a LGTM test ( "it 'looks good to me'"); people will assume it's legit, even when it is isn't.
claim: Deep Research outputs will almost always pass a superficial review (LGTM test) even when they contain errors

quote: Students will quickly learn that every term paper it writes gets an A.
claim: Students will quickly learn that Deep Research can write term papers that receive high grades

quote: Scientific researchers will start to use it, too (as Unutmaz recommended). And inevitably errors will seep in to the scientific literature, often undetected.
claim: Scientific researchers will use Deep Research and errors will inevitably seep into the scientific literature, often undetected

quote: The output of DeepResearch will inevitably feed further models; we will wind up with more of this
claim: Deep Research output will feed back into training data for future models, accelerating model collapse

quote: paper mill factories (there is already such a thing, designed to game citation counts, "an illegitimate, yet lucrative business" that is growing) will massively ramp up production, choking the peer review process and saturating the market with hard-to-find, hard-to-rectify errors
claim: Paper mill factories will massively ramp up production using Deep Research, choking peer review and saturating scientific literature with errors

quote: When — not if — Deep Research begins to flood the medical zone with shite, we are all in trouble.
claim: Deep Research will flood the medical information space with unreliable content, creating serious public health risks

quote: GPT-3 is the king of pastiche.
claim: GPT-3 operates by creating pastiche -- imitating the style and content of human text without understanding it

quote: it is mimic that knows not whereof it speaks
claim: LLMs are mimics that do not understand the content they produce

quote: Knowledge is in part about specific properties of particular entities. GPT's mimicry draws on vast troves of human text that, for example, often put together subjects [England] with predicates [won 5 Eurovision contests].
claim: LLMs draw on statistical associations between subjects and predicates in training text but lose track of precise entity-property bindings

quote: GPT's heavy use of a technique called embeddings makes it really good at substituting synonyms and more broadly related phrases, but the same tendency towards substitution often lead it astray.
claim: LLMs' embedding-based approach makes them good at synonym substitution but the same mechanism leads them to make inappropriate substitutions

quote: It never fully masters abstract relationship.
claim: LLMs never fully master abstract relationships between entities

quote: When GPT gets things right, it is often combining bits that don't belong together, but not quite in random ways, but rather in ways where there is some overlap in some aspect or another.
claim: When LLMs produce correct-seeming output, they are combining fragments that share some surface-level overlap but may not actually belong together

quote: At least for now, it still takes a human to know which plausible bits actually belong together.
claim: Humans are still required to judge which plausible-seeming LLM outputs are actually correct

quote: ChatGPT still seems likes pretty much the same mix of brilliance and stupidity that I wrote about, more than two years ago
claim: ChatGPT in early 2025 still exhibits the same fundamental mix of brilliance and stupidity as two years ago

quote: failed, multiple times, to properly count to 50
claim: ChatGPT fails multiple times at basic tasks like counting to 50 and listing all US states

quote: reported that the letter h could be a vowel, at least when it appeared in the word Northwest
claim: ChatGPT incorrectly classified the letter 'h' as a vowel when appearing in the word 'Northwest'

quote: issued numerous corrections that were wrong, never acknowledging uncertainty until after its errors were called out
claim: ChatGPT issues confident corrections that are themselves wrong and only acknowledges uncertainty after errors are pointed out

quote: if AI can't do Kapoor's expense reports or my simple tables, is AGI really imminent? Who is kidding whom?
claim: AI's inability to reliably handle expense reports and simple table generation demonstrates AGI is not imminent

claim: Google is not yet making significant revenue from AI, and this unsustainable spending will force them to monetize through military and surveillance applications.

quote: pure scaling of LLMs – just adding more data and compute to extant LLM architectures – would not bring us to AGI, and that so-called scaling laws were merely empirical generalizations rather than physical laws
claim: Pure scaling of LLMs by adding more data and compute will not achieve AGI, and scaling laws are empirical generalizations rather than physical laws.

quote: these conclusions are so widely accepted now that Satya Nadella himself recently repeated them, almost word for word
claim: The failure of pure LLM scaling to reach AGI is now widely accepted, including by industry leaders like Satya Nadella.

quote: DeepSeek's R1 model (which OpenAI has acknowledged resembles their test-time inference system o1) explicitly includes a "rule-based reward system" for verifying some classes of answers. Neurosymbolic for the win!
claim: Newer AI models like DeepSeek R1 are already incorporating neurosymbolic techniques (combining neural networks with rule-based systems), validating the neurosymbolic approach.

quote: test-time compute systems are not across the board improvements like GPT-4 was relative to GPT-3 or GPT-3 was relative to GPT-2, but rather improvements in certain domains such as coding and math
claim: Test-time compute systems (like o1/o3/R1) provide improvements only in specific domains like coding and math, not across-the-board improvements like earlier GPT generations.

quote: Even the latest systems like Deep Research are still struggling in a few ways – and those ways pretty much correspond exactly to the places that I warned would be LLM's Achilles' Heels: hallucinations and reasoning errors.
claim: The latest AI systems like Deep Research continue to struggle with hallucinations and reasoning errors, which remain fundamental weaknesses of LLMs.

quote: if the scaling of pure LLMs ran out, you would expect to have multiple teams competing and reaching a point of diminishing returns, with essentially no moat, and a lot of competition over price
claim: The exhaustion of pure LLM scaling leads to commoditization, with multiple competitors reaching similar performance levels, no competitive moat, and price wars.

quote: LLMs, once novel, are largely a commodity.
claim: LLMs have become largely commoditized products.

quote: So much for exponential progress.
claim: LLMs have not made exponential progress on hallucination problems; Google's AI is still making hallucination errors in high-profile contexts, just as it did two years earlier.

trigger: Musk's $97.4B bid for OpenAI nonprofit assets

claim: The US having large LLMs will not prevent China from using its own LLMs for authoritarian purposes.
