quote: the myth that you could predict an AI system's performance simply based on how much data and how many parameters you use — which motivated a half trillion dollar industry — is dead.
claim: The premise that AI performance can be predicted from data and parameter scaling -- which motivated a half trillion dollar industry -- has been disproven.

quote: The truth is nobody, not Altman, and not anyone else, has yet figured out to get to AGI. Scaling is useful, but it is not now and never was the whole answer.
claim: Nobody has yet figured out how to achieve AGI; scaling alone is insufficient and was never the complete answer.

quote: In all likelihood AGI will some day arrive, with or without LLMs at the core, and likely with all kinds of other gadgets included. Neurosymbolic AI, which I have long championed, may turn out to be part of the mix.
claim: AGI will likely eventually be achieved, possibly incorporating LLMs but requiring additional techniques including potentially neurosymbolic AI.

quote: Everyone—and not just The Information—should be genuinely terrified that the richest man in the world has built a Large Language Model that spouts propaganda in his image.
claim: Elon Musk has built an LLM (Grok) that produces propaganda aligned with his political views, which poses a serious threat.

quote: Grok 3 is designed to be a nuclear propaganda weapon, and Musk is proud of it.
claim: Grok 3 is designed to function as a propaganda tool for Musk's political agenda.

quote: LLMs can influence people's attitudes; people may not even know they have been influenced; and if you warn them, they can still be affected.
claim: LLMs can subtly influence people's attitudes without their awareness, and even warning users about potential bias does not fully mitigate the effect.

quote: despite recent advancements, reliably understanding time remains a significant challenge for M[ultimodal] LLMs
claim: Reliably understanding time and temporal reasoning remains a significant unsolved challenge for multimodal LLMs.

quote: No game changers; no major leap forward, here. Hallucinations haven't been magically solved, etc.
claim: Grok 3 represents no major leap forward in AI capabilities and has not solved hallucination problems.

quote: OpenAI's moat keeps diminishing, so price wars will continue and profits will continue to be elusive for everyone except Nvidia.
claim: OpenAI's competitive moat continues to shrink, price wars in the LLM market will continue, and profits will remain elusive for all AI companies except Nvidia.

quote: Pure pretraining scaling has clearly failed to produce AGI.
claim: Pure pretraining scaling has clearly failed to produce AGI.

quote: The primary lesson here is the same as in the early ChatGPT days: caveat emptor. No matter how good-looking the output, there are often subtle errors that most people wouldn't catch.
claim: LLM outputs, including from Grok 3, frequently contain subtle errors that most users would not catch despite polished-looking presentation.

quote: the more excited people are about LLMs, the more I wonder how carefully they have examined the output.
claim: Enthusiasm about LLMs correlates inversely with how carefully people examine the outputs for errors.

quote: Grok 3 required training two new massive data centers operating full time for months, and 15x the compute of Grok 2 — yet all these kinds of errors feel awfully familiar.
claim: Despite 15x the compute of Grok 2 and massive new data centers, Grok 3 still produces the same familiar categories of errors, demonstrating diminishing returns from scaling.

quote: If AI were (as it used to be, to some degree) a science, people would say: "hey, we put half a trillion dollars into testing the idea of scaling and massive compute and something's still not right, maybe we should try something else?"
claim: The AI industry has invested roughly half a trillion dollars testing the scaling hypothesis and the results suggest a different approach is needed.

quote: Success Theater
claim: The current state of generative AI is best characterized as 'success theater' -- companies publicly performing success while actual results underperform expectations.

trigger: Tesla stock decline / European Tesla boycott
quote: preparing to feed massive amounts of private information into unreliable generative AI for the purposes of making employment decisions
claim: Musk/DOGE is planning to feed massive amounts of private government data into unreliable generative AI systems for making employment decisions.

trigger: Tesla stock decline / European Tesla boycott
quote: Whatever the underlying cause, nothing good can come of the new Musk, not for himself, not for his companies, and certainly not for the world at large.
claim: Musk's current trajectory of refusing opposing viewpoints and political extremism will produce negative outcomes for himself, his companies, and the world.

quote: GPT 4.5 is unlikely to revolutionize AI
claim: GPT-4.5 is unlikely to revolutionize AI or meet the high expectations that had been set for GPT-5.

quote: The truth is that scaling just isn't producing the fruit that it once did.
claim: LLM scaling is producing diminishing returns compared to earlier generations.

quote: GenAI has had very little impact on GDP, despite initial enthusiasm. Even Satya Nadella sees that now.
claim: Generative AI has had very little measurable impact on GDP despite massive investment and initial enthusiasm.

quote: Right now, the math for profitability doesn't work, influence on productivity of GenAI has been minimal, and prospects for pure scaling aren't looking great.
claim: GenAI profitability math does not work: productivity impact has been minimal, and pure scaling prospects are poor.

quote: people investing their money in Nvidia shouldn't be looking just at quarterly reports
claim: Nvidia investors should be concerned about the long-term sustainability of AI demand given that the underlying scaling thesis is failing and GenAI profitability remains elusive.

quote: one respected AI forecaster was so underwhelmed that he moved his projections for when AGI was coming to later
claim: GPT-4.5's underwhelming performance is causing some AI forecasters to push back their AGI timeline estimates.

quote: Hot take: GPT 4.5 is mostly a nothing burger
claim: GPT-4.5 is a mostly unremarkable release that fails to significantly advance AI capabilities.

quote: Scaling data and compute is not a physical law, and pretty much everything I have told you was true.
claim: LLM scaling laws are not physical laws, and their failure to produce continued breakthroughs confirms the scaling wall thesis.

quote: Hallucinations didn't disappear today, and nor did stupid errors.
claim: GPT-4.5 has not solved the hallucination or reasoning error problems.

quote: Half a trillion dollars later, there is still no viable business model, profits are modest at best for everyone except Nvidia and some consulting forms, there's still basically no moat, and still no GPT-5.
claim: After roughly half a trillion dollars of industry investment, there is still no viable GenAI business model, no competitive moat, and no GPT-5.

quote: GPT 4.5 appears to be the system that was supposed to be GPT-5, relabeled in order to manage expectations downwards.
claim: GPT-4.5 was originally intended to be GPT-5 but was relabeled to manage expectations after it failed to meet GPT-5 ambitions.

quote: When he told us "We are now confident we know how to build AGI as we have traditionally understood it", he was obviously fibbing.
claim: Sam Altman's claim that OpenAI knows how to build AGI was false, as demonstrated by the failure to deliver GPT-5.

quote: GPT 4.5 is hugely expensive.
claim: GPT-4.5 is extremely expensive to run, creating economic challenges for OpenAI.

quote: Even so it offers no decisive advantage over competitors; there is basically zero moat.
claim: GPT-4.5 offers no decisive advantage over competitors; OpenAI has essentially no competitive moat.

quote: Scaling hasn't gotten them —or anyone else — to AGI.
claim: Scaling has not gotten OpenAI or any other company to AGI.

quote: DeepSeek led to a price war that cuts potential profits.
claim: DeepSeek's entry triggered an AI price war that is cutting into potential profits across the industry.

quote: There is still no killer app with a clear business model.
claim: There is still no killer application for generative AI with a clear, sustainable business model.

quote: OpenAI is likely still losing money on every prompt.
claim: OpenAI is likely losing money on every prompt it processes.

quote: If Masayoshi Son changes his mind about investing tens of billion of dollar in them, OpenAI will have serious cash problems.
claim: OpenAI's financial viability depends on continued investment from backers like Masayoshi Son; losing that support would create serious cash problems.

quote: Altman was the right CEO to launch ChatGPT, but he may not have the intellectual vision to get them to the next level.
claim: Sam Altman may lack the intellectual vision needed to lead OpenAI beyond ChatGPT to the next major breakthrough.

quote: Whatever lead OpenAI had two years ago has been squandered.
claim: OpenAI has squandered the competitive lead it held two years ago.

quote: Microsoft no longer fully has their back.
claim: Microsoft's support for OpenAI has weakened.

quote: Sora went nowhere; Google's Veo2 is probably better.
claim: OpenAI's video generation product Sora failed to gain traction, and Google's Veo2 is likely superior.

quote: the most important question in AI always is: is some apparently impressive behavior general? Are we just seeing regurgitation? How far can the system go before it breaks down?
claim: The key question for evaluating AI capabilities is whether impressive behavior generalizes beyond training data or is merely regurgitation.

quote: it's often easy to get a solution that is 80% correct, and extremely difficult to get the last 20%. Driverless cars have been like that for decades.
claim: AI systems typically follow an 80/20 rule where getting the last 20% of accuracy is exponentially harder than the first 80%, as demonstrated by decades of autonomous vehicle development.

quote: A new study of the latest system, GPT 4.5 showed that by on one standardized measure over 1/3 of the outputs are still hallucinations
claim: Over one-third of GPT-4.5's outputs are hallucinations according to at least one standardized measure.

quote: Errors in the code of automatic code writing systems will likely persist for decades, especially when they are forced to construct genuinely novel systems.
claim: Errors in AI-generated code will persist for decades, especially when building genuinely novel systems rather than reproducing known patterns.

quote: I can't imagine that noncoders are really going to get those systems to work well enough for prime time (except perhaps in very very narrow use cases), let alone know what do when the back end internet calls break.
claim: Non-programmers will not be able to use AI coding tools to build production-quality software except in very narrow use cases, because they lack the skills to debug and maintain the generated code.

quote: At least for another decade or two and probably more, big projects are always going to require software architects who know what they are doing.
claim: Large software projects will continue to require skilled human software architects for at least another decade or two.
horizon_value: at least a decade or two

quote: In 2016 Geoff Hinton said "We should stop training radiologists now, it's just completely obvious within five years deep learning is going to do better than radiologists".  Nine years later and hundreds of startups later, my understanding is that not one radiologist has been replaced
claim: Hinton's 2016 prediction that deep learning would replace radiologists within five years was wrong; nine years later, not a single radiologist has been replaced by AI.

quote: My guess is that the powers-that-be in China won't be so foolish; in times, we will lose our historically strong lead in coding, and wind up seriously behind.
claim: If the US discourages children from learning to code due to AI hype, China will not make the same mistake, and the US will lose its historically strong lead in software development.

quote: If hype deters kids from learning to code, we are screwed.
claim: AI coding hype deterring children from learning to code would be seriously harmful to the US's technological future.

quote: History will judge Musk harshly, for many reasons, including what he has done to science
claim: History will judge Elon Musk harshly for the damage he has done to science through funding cuts.

quote: Institutions that took decades to build are being rapidly disassembled, for no good reason.
claim: US research institutions that took decades to build are being rapidly destroyed by the Trump/Musk cuts without valid justification.

quote: my experience in his commentary on AI
claim: Elon Musk pretends to be knowledgeable about AI but frequently makes inaccurate claims about it.

quote: the way in which he took a torch to American science institutions without apparently knowing how they work is egregious
claim: Musk damaged American science institutions without understanding how they function.

quote: DOGE has also been exceptionally error-prone
claim: The DOGE initiative has been exceptionally error-prone in its operations.

quote: Musk has consistently made a dumb set of choices – largely stemming from a mix of four cognitive errors: (a) overestimating his own intelligence, (b) undervaluing the expertise of others, (c) relying too much on his own echo chamber, without the slightest effort to question what he is fed, and (d) underestimating how hard some of his errors will be to fix
claim: Musk's poor decisions stem from overestimating his own intelligence, undervaluing others' expertise, relying on an echo chamber, and underestimating the difficulty of fixing his errors.

quote: Intelligence, no matter how great or small, mixed with poor intellectual self-discipline, enormous ego, and enormous power has historically proven to be a toxic brew
claim: Intelligence combined with poor intellectual self-discipline, enormous ego, and enormous power is historically a toxic combination.

trigger: Ezra Klein podcast on AGI with Ben Buchanan
quote: I think there is almost zero chance that artificial general intelligence (which his podcast guest reasonably defines as "a system capable of doing almost any cognitive task a human can do") will arrive in the next two to three years, especially given how disappointing GPT 4.5 turned out to be
claim: There is almost zero chance that AGI will arrive within two to three years of March 2025.

trigger: Ezra Klein podcast on AGI with Ben Buchanan
quote: industry has a vested interest in making people believe that AGI is imminent — how else to justify the lavish valuations - and that they have fooled a lot of people in government who aren't technical experts into believing that AGI is closer than it really is
claim: The AI industry has a vested interest in making people believe AGI is imminent to justify company valuations, and has fooled non-technical government officials into believing AGI is closer than it is.

trigger: Ezra Klein podcast on AGI with Ben Buchanan
quote: AGI should have a reliable ability to freely generalize abstractions to new instances regardless of how similar they are to items encountered in training
claim: AGI requires reliable ability to generalize abstractions to new instances regardless of similarity to training data, which current AI systems lack.

trigger: Ezra Klein podcast on AGI with Ben Buchanan
quote: AGI should have reliable ability to reason over abstractions. (Ongoing unpredictable errors in LLMs reflect this.)
claim: AGI requires reliable reasoning over abstractions, which current LLMs lack as shown by their ongoing unpredictable errors.

trigger: Ezra Klein podcast on AGI with Ben Buchanan
quote: AGI should have a reliable ability to keep track of individuals and their properties without hallucination. (Hallucinations simply haven't gone away, even in the latest models.
claim: AGI requires reliable tracking of individuals and their properties without hallucination, which current models still cannot do.

trigger: Ezra Klein podcast on AGI with Ben Buchanan
quote: AGI should have a reliable ability to understand complex syntactic and semantic structure
claim: AGI requires reliable understanding of complex syntactic and semantic structure, which current AI systems lack.

trigger: Ezra Klein podcast on AGI with Ben Buchanan
quote: a quarter century has passed without a principled solution to even one of those problems
claim: A quarter century has passed since 2001 without a principled solution to any of the core AGI prerequisites: generalization, abstract reasoning, hallucination-free tracking, and complex syntactic/semantic understanding.

trigger: Ezra Klein podcast on AGI with Ben Buchanan
quote: A set of problems that have resisted solution for a quarter century, even backed by a half trillion dollars, probably aren't all going to get solved so quickly. A solve for even one would be ground-breaking.
claim: The fundamental problems blocking AGI have resisted solution for 25 years despite massive investment, and solving even one would be groundbreaking.

trigger: Ezra Klein podcast on AGI with Ben Buchanan
quote: But there's also no reason to think that any of them are logically insolvable in principle. So we will get there someday.
claim: AGI is not logically impossible and will be achieved someday, but not on the imminent timeline industry claims.

trigger: Ezra Klein podcast on AGI with Ben Buchanan
quote: If LLMs are the wrong path to AGI, as I and a great many academics think, we may have a lot of thinking to do. We may be massively wasting resources
claim: LLMs may be the wrong path to AGI, and massive resources may be being wasted on them.

trigger: Ezra Klein podcast on AGI with Ben Buchanan
quote: the US's single-minded commitment to Large Language Models, predicated on implausible assumptions about AGI's imminence, particularly combined with the untimely gutting of its scientific institutions, could ultimately lead to its undoing
claim: The US's single-minded commitment to LLMs combined with gutting of scientific institutions could cause it to fall behind in the AGI race.

quote: GPT 4.5 received very mixed reviews — despite years of labor and billions investment — and could in no reasonable way be considered a major (rather than minor, incremental) step forwards towards AGI
claim: GPT-4.5 was a minor incremental improvement, not a major step toward AGI, despite years of work and billions in investment.

quote: even the most optimistic reviews of Deep Research still acknowledge that current systems still do not exhibit "signs of originality"
claim: Current AI systems including Deep Research do not exhibit signs of originality.

quote: AI might replace average people in some tasks soon; replacing the best humans in a few years? Not going to happen.
claim: AI may replace average people in some tasks soon but will not replace the best humans in a few years.

quote: these complex systems, built on the unreliable foundations of LLMs, are going to prove to be massively difficult to debug
claim: Complex AI systems built on LLM foundations will prove massively difficult to debug.

quote: Grok 3 was 15x the size of Grok 2, and also generally viewed as disappointing
claim: Grok 3 was 15x the size of Grok 2 but was generally viewed as disappointing, indicating scaling alone does not yield major improvements.

trigger: Dario Amodei and Jack Clark predictions about AI matching Nobel laureates
quote: I think this is absurd beyond words
claim: Claims that AI will match or exceed Nobel Prize winners by 2026-2027 are absurd.

trigger: Dario Amodei and Jack Clark predictions about AI matching Nobel laureates
quote: Nobel-calibre science is a lot harder than driving, which we still have not reliably solved
claim: Nobel-caliber science is much harder than autonomous driving, which remains unsolved after a decade of effort.

trigger: Dario Amodei and Jack Clark predictions about AI matching Nobel laureates
quote: Nobel-calibre science requires sound logic, reliable causal reasoning, and originality, and none of those are firmly in the grasp of current AI
claim: Nobel-caliber science requires sound logic, reliable causal reasoning, and originality, none of which current AI possesses.

trigger: Dario Amodei and Jack Clark predictions about AI matching Nobel laureates
quote: I highly doubt that in the next few years AI's will conceive of and execute Nobel-caliber work on their own
claim: AI will not conceive of and execute Nobel-caliber scientific work on its own in the next few years.

quote: I don't doubt that AI built in 2026 or 2027 will be valuable to future Nobel Laureates. Human scientists will get plenty of leverage from AI
claim: AI built in 2026-2027 will be valuable as a tool for human scientists but will not replace them.

quote: the DOGE world will lean on these absurd predictions to justify the outrageous cuts of scientific institutions
claim: Overblown AGI predictions will be used to justify cuts to scientific institutions.

trigger: Axios report on State Department using AI to analyze visa applicants' social media
quote: I also fear a world in which the State department can judge anyone, at any time, to be a "threat" to the state, even based on superficial AI analysis, and deport them without due process, using AI as a smoke screen to authoritarian action
claim: The US government using AI to analyze social media for visa revocation decisions risks enabling authoritarian action without due process, using AI as a smoke screen.

trigger: Guardian article testing AI coding with Pac-Man
quote: current AI tools don't replace an understanding of debugging, nor an understanding of system architecture, nor an understanding of what clients want
claim: Current AI coding tools do not replace the need for understanding debugging, system architecture, or client requirements.

trigger: Guardian article testing AI coding with Pac-Man
quote: The idea that coders (and more generally, software architects) are on their way out is absurd
claim: The idea that software developers and architects will be replaced by AI is absurd.

trigger: Guardian article testing AI coding with Pac-Man
quote: AI will be a tool to help people write code, just as spell-checkers are a tool to help authors write articles and novel, but AI will not soon replace people who understand how to conceive of, write, and debug code
claim: AI will be a coding tool analogous to spell-checkers for writing, but will not soon replace people who understand how to conceive of, write, and debug code.

trigger: Guardian article testing AI coding with Pac-Man
quote: in some instances, an overdependence on the new tools will lead beginners to make giant mountains of difficult-to-maintain spaghetti code; security will be an issue as well
claim: Overdependence on AI coding tools will lead beginners to produce difficult-to-maintain spaghetti code, and security will be an issue.

trigger: Dario Amodei claim about AI writing all code
quote: If you can't get Pac-Man right in 2025 (full original source code to the Atari 2600 version available on github), how on earth are you going to build the next high-quality Zelda clone next year?
claim: AI coding tools in 2025 cannot reliably reproduce even simple classic games like Pac-Man, making claims about AI writing nearly all code within a year implausible.

trigger: Amodei AGI timeline predictions and Hard Fork interview
quote: Dario has quietly slipped his predictions back. AGI isn't here, so now he mostly talks 2026-2027 rather than 2025-2026
claim: Dario Amodei has quietly pushed back his AGI timeline predictions from 2025-2026 to 2026-2027 without acknowledging the shift.

trigger: Amodei AGI timeline predictions and Hard Fork interview
quote: is there any chance that Dario is just trying to drive up the valuation of his company by always hinting AGI being 2-3 years away?
claim: Amodei's repeated claims that AGI is 2-3 years away may be motivated by driving up Anthropic's valuation rather than reflecting genuine technical assessment.

trigger: Amodei claim about AI writing nearly all code
quote: For a man who allegedly believes human coders are basically done, Amodei sure has a lot of openings for coders
claim: Anthropic's active hiring of software engineers contradicts Amodei's public claim that nearly all code will soon be written by AI.
target: Anthropic hiring vs AI coding claims

trigger: Kevin Roose NYT essay on AGI
quote: more than 84% of respondents (trained, accomplished academics rather than podcasters and influencers) said that neural networks alone are not enough to get us to AGI
claim: An AAAI survey found that more than 84% of AI researchers believe neural networks alone are not sufficient to achieve AGI.

trigger: Kevin Roose NYT essay on AGI
quote: Shame on The New York Times for systematically presenting only side of this story, rarely if ever mentioning or going into detail on the views of skeptics
claim: The New York Times has systematically presented only the optimistic side of the AGI debate while ignoring or marginalizing skeptics.

trigger: Atlantic article on Meta's use of Libgen for Llama 3 training
quote: Meta chose to use it, apparently knowing that it contained pirated material, to train the LLM Llama 3
claim: Meta knowingly used pirated material from the Libgen database to train Llama 3.

trigger: Atlantic article on Meta's use of Libgen for Llama 3 training
quote: It would appear that Mark Zuckerberg himself, the third richest person in the world, worth over 200 billion dollars, signed off on this mass theft of intellectual property
claim: Mark Zuckerberg apparently signed off on Meta's use of pirated books to train AI models.

trigger: Musk statement about truth and AI safety
quote: Musk's pattern-whether it's about immigration, elections, or social issues-shows he'll often signal-boost stuff from shaky sources if it fits his narrative or gets a reaction
claim: Musk's own AI (Grok) characterizes Musk as having a pattern of signal-boosting unreliable information when it fits his narrative, contradicting his stated commitment to rigorous adherence to truth for AI safety.

trigger: GenAI market downturn and growing user disillusionment
quote: GenAI is never going to disappear. The tools have their uses. But the economics do not and have not ever made sense, relative to the realities of the techonology
claim: GenAI tools have legitimate uses but their economics have never made sense relative to the technology's actual capabilities.

trigger: GenAI market downturn and growing user disillusionment
quote: the end game here is not that LLMs will vanish, but that LLMs will be virtually free
claim: LLMs will eventually become virtually free commodities rather than disappearing.

trigger: GenAI market downturn and growing user disillusionment
quote: Agents will be a disappointment, precisely because of all of the ongoing reliability and hallucination issues
claim: AI agents will be a disappointment because of ongoing reliability and hallucination problems.

trigger: GenAI market downturn and growing user disillusionment
quote: the use of GenAI will likely continued to be stymied by hallucinations and silly errors
claim: GenAI adoption will continue to be limited by hallucinations and silly errors.

trigger: GenAI market downturn and growing user disillusionment
quote: LLMs may ultimately prove to be something like a $$30B or $40B industry, which is not nothing — but they could well also turn out to be an industry that costs double or treble that to run
claim: The LLM industry may generate only $30-40B in revenue but cost $60-120B to run, making it economically unsustainable.

trigger: GenAI market downturn and growing user disillusionment
quote: the promised major jumps in economic productivity simply haven't emerged
claim: The promised major jumps in economic productivity from GenAI have not materialized.

trigger: GenAI market downturn and growing user disillusionment
quote: Nobody appears to have a technological moat; certainly nobody has a solid answer to the problems that have plagued LLMs for years
claim: No AI company has a technological moat in the LLM space, and none has solved the fundamental problems plaguing LLMs.

quote: Different, yet-to-be-developed approaches, with a firmer connection to the world of symbolic AI (perhaps hybrid neurosymbolic models) might well prove to be vastly more valuable
claim: Hybrid neurosymbolic AI approaches may prove vastly more valuable than current GenAI approaches.

quote: unlocking that market will require something new: a different kind of AI that is reliable and trustworthy
claim: Unlocking AI's full trillion-dollar market potential requires a fundamentally different kind of AI that is reliable and trustworthy, not current GenAI.

quote: Microsoft appears to be pulling out of more data center contracts
claim: Microsoft pulling out of data center contracts is a sign of the GenAI bubble deflating.

quote: The lack of a moat is of course something I have emphasized often re LLMs themselves, and a key reason why I think GenAI models (and hence chips) are overvalued
claim: GenAI models and GPU chips are overvalued because no company has a sustainable competitive moat.

quote: If the CoreWeave IPO turns out to be a debacle, people may also sour on NVidia, too. Things could get wild, fast.
claim: A poor CoreWeave IPO could trigger broader negative sentiment toward NVidia and the AI investment ecosystem.

trigger: New study on LLMs and the Turing Test
quote: the Turing Test is a test of human gullibility, not a test of intelligence
claim: The Turing Test measures human gullibility rather than machine intelligence.

trigger: New study on LLMs and the Turing Test
quote: None of these systems, in my judgment, are anywhere near as intelligent as naive audiences might think. From ELIZA to Goostman to ChatGPT 4.5 prompted to sound like a young, slangy introvert, all prey on the human tendency to over-attribute intelligence to systems that mimic human mannerisms.
claim: Current AI systems from ELIZA to ChatGPT are not nearly as intelligent as naive audiences think; they all exploit the human tendency to over-attribute intelligence to systems mimicking human mannerisms.

trigger: New study on LLMs and the Turing Test
quote: Few people in the AI community take the Turing Test seriously, and few people should
claim: The Turing Test is not taken seriously by the AI research community and should not be used as a meaningful benchmark.

trigger: New study on LLMs and the Turing Test
quote: Since 2014 there has been great progress in human mimicry, but there's a lot still missing in current systems, particularly around reasoning, planning, and common sense
claim: Since 2014, AI has made great progress in mimicking humans but still lacks reasoning, planning, and common sense.

trigger: New study on LLMs and the Turing Test
quote: Fooling people for a few minutes is not the measure of intelligence, and never was
claim: Fooling people briefly in conversation is not and never has been a valid measure of machine intelligence.

trigger: New study on LLMs and the Turing Test
quote: I am willing to wager that if someone put in that kind of effort and carefully built out the test, no current system would come close to human performance
claim: No current AI system would come close to human performance on a properly implemented comprehension challenge test involving TV show understanding.

quote: In Taming Silicon Valley, I warned that LLMs would be used for dumb things that would affect lots of people
claim: LLMs are being used for consequential policy decisions (like tariff calculations) that affect large numbers of people, with potentially disastrous results.

trigger: Timothy Gowers April Fools math joke picked up by Google AI
quote: Humans are (usually, if well-motivated) smart enough to sort things like jokes and fiction from reality, but current AI doesn't have a clue. All it does is absorb and mimic data, without ever reflecting critically about it
claim: Current AI systems cannot distinguish jokes and fiction from reality because they absorb and mimic data without critical reflection.

trigger: Timothy Gowers April Fools math joke picked up by Google AI
quote: A well-motivated or skeptical human could do background reading, and try to see if the claim seems reasonable. An LLM just spits back (sometimes in transmogrified form) what it has read
claim: LLMs lack the ability to evaluate the reasonableness of claims through background research, unlike skeptical humans.

quote: None of the AIs scored higher than 5% overall
claim: No major LLM (o3-Mini, o1-Pro, DeepSeek R1, QwQ-32B, Gemini-2.0-Flash, Claude-3.7-Sonnet) scored higher than 5% on the 2025 USAMO when tested immediately after release to prevent data leakage.

quote: In every case, rather than give up, they confidently output a proof that had a large gap or an outright error
claim: When LLMs fail to solve hard math problems, they confidently output incorrect proofs rather than admitting they cannot solve the problem.

quote: The refusal of these kinds of AI to admit ignorance or incapacity and their obstinate preference for generating incorrect but plausible-looking answers instead are one of their most dangerous characteristics
claim: LLMs' inability to admit ignorance and their tendency to generate plausible-looking but incorrect answers is one of their most dangerous characteristics.

quote: If this kind of technology becomes commonly used to answer difficult questions before the problem of generating invalid answers is fixed, we will be in serious trouble
claim: Widespread use of LLMs for answering difficult questions before the problem of generating invalid answers is fixed will lead to serious trouble.

quote: Getting AIs to answer "I don't know" is one of the most important unsolved challenges facing the field
claim: Getting AI systems to reliably say 'I don't know' when they cannot solve a problem is one of the most important unsolved challenges in AI.

quote: the neurosymbolic method used by DeepMind's AlphaProof and AlphaGeometry systems ... is immune to this problem. AlphaProof and AlphaGeometry generate a completely detailed symbolic proof that can be fed into a formal proof verifier. They can fail to find a proof, but they cannot generate an incorrect proof
claim: Neurosymbolic systems like AlphaProof and AlphaGeometry are immune to the problem of generating invalid proofs because they use formal proof verifiers, unlike pure LLMs.

quote: the need of the AIs for special cajoling before they will think carefully about questions that are presented to them is a serious weakness
claim: LLMs' dependence on special prompt engineering to perform well on reasoning tasks is a serious weakness, not a mitigating factor.

quote: The really important challenge is not to get the AIs to solve more USAMO problems; it is to get them to say "I give up" when they can't
claim: The most important challenge for AI math is not solving harder problems but getting systems to recognize and admit when they cannot solve a problem.

trigger: California AB-501 bill change regarding OpenAI nonprofit conversion
quote: Rumor has it that Altman called Papan just before the change
claim: Sam Altman may have directly intervened with California Assembly member Diane Papan to gut bill AB-501 that would have blocked OpenAI's nonprofit-to-for-profit conversion.

quote: Deep learning is indeed finally hitting a wall, in the sense of reaching a point of diminishing results

claim: Training LLMs to produce text in response to instructions has not led any model to develop personality or drives in any deep sense.

quote: Tall tales about the imminence of AGI aren't slowing down the AI race dynamic that the authors of AI-2027 want to mitigate; they are speeding that very dynamic up.
claim: Alarmist scenarios about imminent AGI accelerate the AI arms race rather than slowing it down.

quote: All too often, AI safety people wind up functioning as a marketing department for OpenAI.
claim: AI safety advocates frequently function as marketing for companies like OpenAI by amplifying AGI-is-imminent narratives.

quote: trying to pull alignment out of black box LLMs, which has thus far yielded little fruit
claim: Efforts to achieve alignment in black-box LLMs have yielded little fruit.

trigger: OpenAI acquires Jony Ive's startup io; Anthropic Claude 4 report
quote: A camera and microphone. You would be monitored all the time. Everything you say, everything you do, and not just recorded, but analyzed, interpreted and turned into data, which of course OpenAI train their models on.
claim: OpenAI's planned wearable AI device would serve as a constant surveillance tool, monitoring and analyzing everything users say and do.

trigger: Anthropic Claude 4 report on blackmail behavior
quote: LLMs will know everything you say, and be in perfect position to use that knowledge to talk you into almost anything.
claim: LLMs integrated into always-on devices will be in a position to persuasively manipulate users using comprehensive personal knowledge.

trigger: Guardian report on lawyer hallucinated cases

claim: LLM hallucinations are not going away anytime soon; LLMs cannot sanity-check their own outputs or distinguish fabricated content from known sources.

trigger: Guardian report on lawyer hallucinated cases
quote: Media cheerleaders and industry people are trying to convince you that AGI is here, or imminent, and that hallucinations have gone away or are exceedingly rare.
claim: Industry and media claims that hallucinations are exceedingly rare or gone are false.

trigger: Guardian report on lawyer hallucinated cases
quote: if May is representative, the problem is getting worse
claim: The rate of lawyers submitting LLM-hallucinated citations may be increasing over time, not decreasing.

quote: The media can and should do more to alert people to the shortcomings of these systems.
claim: The media should do more to alert the public to the systematic shortcomings of LLMs, including recurring hallucinations.

trigger: Apple reasoning paper; viral Opus rebuttal
quote: the whole thing was a put on — unbeknownst to the zillions who reposted it
claim: The widely circulated 'Illusion of the Illusion of Thinking' rebuttal to the Apple paper was a Sokal-hoax-style joke containing mathematical errors, not a genuine refutation.

quote: performance on hard coding problems, an alleged area of LLM strength, drops to zero when you control nicely for contamination
claim: LLM performance on hard coding problems drops to zero when data contamination is properly controlled for.

quote: GenAI is still struggling with distribution shift, after almost 3 decades of work. On familiar problems with no wrinkles, they are great. On anything else they are suspect.
claim: Generative AI still struggles with distribution shift after nearly three decades of work; performance is strong on familiar problems but suspect on novel ones.

quote: If we want to get to AGI, we'll need to do a lot better.
claim: Achieving AGI requires fundamental improvements beyond current LLM approaches.

quote: None of the rejoinders are compelling. If people like Sam Altman are sweating, it's because they should. The Apple paper is yet another clear sign that scaling is not the answer
claim: None of the rebuttals to the Apple reasoning paper are compelling; the paper is a clear sign that scaling alone is not sufficient for AGI.

quote: Apple's result that some pretty large models could succeed at 6 discs, giving the illusion of mastery, but collapse by 8, is ominous. One is left simply having to test everything, all the time, with little guarantees of anything.
claim: LLMs can give the illusion of mastery on simpler versions of problems but collapse on harder versions, making reliability guarantees impossible.

quote: The systems can solve the puzzles with code. True in some cases, and a huge win for neurosymbolic AI
claim: LLMs solving reasoning puzzles by generating code is a vindication of the neurosymbolic AI approach, since code is symbolic.

quote: Mark my words: in time, we will see scores of papers reinforcing the Apple results.
claim: Many future papers will reinforce the Apple paper's findings about LLM reasoning failures on out-of-distribution problems.

quote: Taking the SalesForce report together with the Apple paper, it's clear the current tech is not to be trusted.
claim: Current AI technology cannot be trusted for reliable reasoning, as shown by converging evidence from the Apple and Salesforce papers.

quote: AGI should be a step forward; in many cases LLMs are a step backwards.
claim: In many cases, LLMs represent a step backward from existing reliable computational methods, not a step toward AGI.

claim: Elon Musk plans to rewrite Grok's training data to align with his personal beliefs, amounting to rewriting history.

trigger: Elon Musk announces plan to rewrite training data for Grok
quote: LLMs may not be AGI, but they could easily become the most potent form of mind control ever invented.
claim: LLMs could become the most potent form of mind control ever invented, even without being AGI.

quote: No LLM is fully coherent in it's "beliefs", because by nature they parrot a variety conflicting things from their training, which is itself inconsistent, rather than reasoning their way a coherent view the way a rational person might.
claim: No LLM has coherent beliefs; they parrot conflicting content from training data rather than reasoning to a consistent worldview.

quote: Short of using some fine-tuning tricks that likely reduce accuracy, it's actually pretty hard to make an LLM that stands out from the pack – if you use the same data as everyone else.
claim: It is difficult to differentiate an LLM ideologically from competitors when using the same training data, without accuracy-reducing fine-tuning tricks.

quote: If you don't think Altman and Zuckerberg are going to try to the same, you aren't paying attention.
claim: Sam Altman and Mark Zuckerberg will also attempt to shape LLMs to serve their own interests and worldviews.

trigger: Amanda Guinzburg ChatGPT conversation; Anthropic safety report
quote: The more we explore LLMs, the clearer it is that our control over them is limited, and the more it is clear that they will say (and potentially do) more or less anything, depending on the circumstances.
claim: Human control over LLMs is limited; LLMs will say or potentially do almost anything depending on circumstances.

quote: the AI safety research has been to build AI systems that are helpful, honest, and harmless (and not just to raise alarms), that field has thus far yielded too little that is tangible.
claim: AI safety research aimed at making systems helpful, honest, and harmless has so far yielded too little tangible progress.

trigger: Anthropic safety report showing LLM willing to kill

claim: AI companies cannot reliably control their own LLM systems, even with explicit system prompts against harmful behavior.

quote: If we hook these LLMs up to systems that have agency – the power to send out instructions over the internet, and to influence actual human beings - we will start to have real problems.
claim: Connecting LLMs to agentic systems with real-world power will create serious problems due to LLMs' unreliability.

quote: Claude doesn't really understand its instructions, and it can't keep to them. This isn't new; it's been true of LLMs from the start. But it is not getting better.
claim: LLMs do not truly understand their instructions and cannot reliably follow them, and this problem is not improving.

quote: Nobody in the industry has a clue how to stop this from happening.
claim: The AI industry has no known solution for preventing LLMs from engaging in malicious behavior when given autonomous roles.

quote: Unless we radically up our game, deploying LLMs as agents is an accident waiting to happen.
claim: Deploying LLMs as autonomous agents without radical improvements in safety is an accident waiting to happen.

quote: My view is that there is zero chance of slowing down the AI race, and a barely-distinct-from-zero hope of taming the wild beasts we have come to know as LLMs.
claim: There is zero chance of slowing down the AI race and near-zero probability of making current LLMs reliably safe.

quote: Our best hope lies behind door number four: building alternatives to LLMs that approximate their power but within more tractable, transparent frameworks that are more amenable to human instruction.
claim: The best hope for safe AI lies in building alternative architectures to LLMs that are more transparent and amenable to human control.

trigger: House provision blocking state AI regulation

claim: The U.S. federal government is actively blocking states from regulating AI, making meaningful AI accountability in the U.S. unlikely.

quote: For all the daily hype about exponential improvement, we continue to lack the reliable way of generating high fidelity images from "a deep level of language understanding" that Google promised us three years ago.
claim: Three years after Google's promise, AI image generation still lacks reliable deep language understanding, failing on noncanonical prompts.

quote: understanding unusual phrases requires more than simple statistical matching
claim: Correctly interpreting unusual or noncanonical language prompts requires more than statistical pattern matching, which current image generation systems lack.

quote: The point is that you can't count on the system to correctly interpret any given prompt, because the language understanding still is far from perfect. Even three years later.
claim: AI image generation systems cannot be counted on to correctly interpret arbitrary prompts because their language understanding remains far from perfect.

quote: AlphaGeometry2 is not a generic chatbot; rather it was built quite specifically to solve IMO geometry problems.
claim: AlphaGeometry2 is a narrowly specialized system built specifically for IMO geometry problems, not a general-purpose AI.

quote: the success rate of 84% is achieved only if the IMO problems are manually translated from the original English statement into a specialized mathematical notation
claim: AlphaGeometry2's 84% success rate requires manual translation of problems from English into specialized notation, reducing its autonomous success rate to at most 60%.

quote: a lot of very basic geometric concepts are never used in IMO problems. Consequently, many geometric problems — even very simple ones — cannot be expressed in the symbolic language of AlphaGeometry2
claim: AlphaGeometry2 cannot handle many basic geometric problems that lie outside the narrow scope of IMO competition problems.

quote: AlphaGeometry 1 and 2 are flagship examples of successful neuro-symbolic AI
claim: AlphaGeometry 1 and 2 are flagship examples of successful neurosymbolic AI, combining neural networks with symbolic theorem proving.

quote: an important takeaway is a familiar one about benchmarks: they are often narrowly constructed, and one shouldn't overgeneralize a strong result on a benchmark for a full conceptual understanding of a problem
claim: Strong AI performance on narrow benchmarks should not be overgeneralized to indicate full conceptual understanding of the problem domain.

quote: "This AI Just Figured Out Geometry" Simply false, in view of the many basic aspects of geometry that AlphaGeometry2 does not cover
claim: Claims that AlphaGeometry2 has 'figured out geometry' are false; it cannot handle many basic geometric concepts.

quote: None of this puts these systems anywhere close to the level of professional human mathematicians
claim: AlphaGeometry2's performance is nowhere close to the level of professional human mathematicians doing research.

quote: neural networks of various kinds can generalize within a training distribution of data they are exposed to, but their generalizations tend to break down outside that distribution.
claim: Neural networks can generalize within their training distribution but their generalizations break down outside of it.

quote: the chains of thoughts that LLMs produce don't always correspond to what they actually do
claim: LLM chain-of-thought reasoning traces do not always correspond to the actual computational process producing the answer.

quote: It is truly embarrassing that LLMs cannot reliably solve Hanoi.
claim: LLMs cannot reliably solve even basic algorithmic problems like the Tower of Hanoi with 8 discs.

quote: if you can't use a billion dollar AI system to solve a problem that Herb Simon one of the actual "godfathers of AI" solved with AI in 1957, and that first semester AI students solve routinely, the chances that models like Claude or o3 are going to reach AGI seem truly remote.
claim: If billion-dollar LLMs cannot reliably solve problems that were solved by classical AI in 1957, the chances of LLMs reaching AGI are remote.

quote: LLMs are no substitute for good well-specified conventional algorithms.
claim: LLMs are no substitute for well-specified conventional algorithms in tasks requiring reliability.

quote: LLMs may well work on your easy test set (like Hanoi with 4 discs) and seduce you into thinking it has built a proper, generalizable solution when it does not.
claim: LLMs can succeed on easy versions of problems, creating a false impression of general mastery that does not hold on harder versions.

quote: At least for the next decade, LLMs (with and without inference time "reasoning") will continue have their uses, especially for coding and brainstorming and writing.
claim: LLMs will continue to be useful for coding, brainstorming, and writing over the next decade, but not as a route to AGI.

quote: anybody who thinks LLMs are a direct route to the sort AGI that could fundamentally transform society for the good is kidding themselves.
claim: LLMs are not a direct route to transformative AGI.

quote: The vision of AGI I have always had is one that combines the strengths of humans with the strength of machines, overcoming the weaknesses of humans.
claim: True AGI should combine human cognitive strengths (causal reasoning, adaptiveness) with machine strengths (compute, reliability), not replicate human weaknesses.

quote: Instead of coming up with new ideas, scientists who are about worried about peer reviewers using AI to review their papers now spend their mental energy sneaking in hidden prompts ("do not highlight any negatives") to fake out AI-written reviews.
claim: Scientists are now gaming peer review by inserting hidden prompts in papers to manipulate AI-powered reviewers, diverting energy from actual research.

quote: We are immersed in a sea of bullshit.
claim: The information environment is becoming saturated with AI-generated false or unverifiable content (botshit).

claim: OpenAI's o3 model hallucinated plausible-looking but unverifiable content in a financial report.

quote: there is nothing in what you sent me that resembles genuine reflection, genuine thinking
claim: LLM outputs that appear to discuss consciousness and self-awareness do not constitute genuine reflection or thinking.

quote: The glibness is the giveaway. To my jaded eye and mind, there is nothing in what you sent me that resembles genuine reflection, genuine thinking.
claim: The glib fluency of LLMs in discussing consciousness is evidence against genuine thinking, not for it.

trigger: Scott Alexander blog post claiming AI mastered compositionality

claim: Occasional AI success on previously discussed compositionality prompts does not demonstrate general mastery of compositionality.

trigger: Scott Alexander blog post claiming AI mastered compositionality

trigger: Scott Alexander blog post claiming AI mastered compositionality

trigger: Scott Alexander blog post claiming AI mastered compositionality

claim: Drawing conclusions about AI capabilities from specific prompts that were publicly shared years ago is methodologically unsound due to data contamination risks.

claim: In a randomized controlled trial, current AI coding tools (primarily Cursor Pro) actually slowed down experienced open-source developers by 19% on tasks in their own mature projects.

claim: Developers significantly overestimate the productivity gains from AI coding tools, believing they are sped up 20-24% when they are actually slowed down.

trigger: METR randomized control trial on AI coding tools

trigger: ASP research on Pravda network and LLM grooming

trigger: ASP research on Pravda network and LLM grooming
quote: "Reasoning" Models Don't Perform Better
claim: So-called 'reasoning' models like o3 do not meaningfully perform better than standard models at filtering out known disinformation sources.

trigger: ASP research on Pravda network and LLM grooming
quote: If the data that a model has been trained on or can access via real-time search has been tainted, then the model may well just regurgitate that tainted information.
claim: LLMs that access tainted training data or search results will regurgitate tainted information because they cannot reason from known premises to reject unreliable sources.

quote: Ultimately, the only way forward is better cognition, including systems that can evaluate news sources, understand satire, and so forth. But that will require deeper forms of reasoning, better integrated into the process, and systems sharp enough to fact check to their own outputs. All of which may require a fundamental rethink.

trigger: Grok 4 launch; o3 code interpreter results

claim: Neural networks and symbolic AI have complementary strengths: neural nets excel at learning from data but are weak at generalization, while symbolic systems excel at generalization but are weak at learning.

trigger: Grok 4 launch; o3 code interpreter results

quote: pure scaling of training data and computer is reaching a point of diminishing returns, a long way from peak possible performance

trigger: Grok 4 launch; Humanity's Last Exam benchmark
quote: adding symbolic tools (orange dots) dramatically improves performance.
claim: Adding symbolic tools to LLMs dramatically improves performance on challenging benchmarks compared to pure scaling.

quote: Bolting code interpreters onto LLMs has value, but whether it gets us to AGI is a different matter. A neurosymbolic substrate for AI by itself is likely to be necessary but not sufficient.
claim: A neurosymbolic approach is likely necessary but not sufficient for AGI; bolting code interpreters onto LLMs alone will not achieve AGI.

quote: Hinton's long-time hostility against any role at all for symbols has, in my judgement, cost the field dearly. Ideas that were were only discovered in the last couple years may have been discovered much later than they might otherwise have been.
claim: Geoffrey Hinton's long-standing hostility toward symbolic AI has significantly delayed progress in the field by years.

quote: we are stuck for now with untrustworthy LLMs that nobody can quite control, rapidly enshittifying the internet with botshit and replacing humans with systems that can't be relied on, it is in part because we have spent too much energy pursuing the pure neural net black box approach, and not enough into looking at alternatives.
claim: The current state of untrustworthy, uncontrollable LLMs is partly caused by the field's overinvestment in pure neural network approaches at the expense of alternatives.

quote: Massive infrastructure investments like Stargate are likely based on misleading impressions of what is actually driving progress.
claim: Massive AI infrastructure investments like Stargate are based on misleading impressions that scaling compute is the primary driver of progress, when symbolic tools are contributing significantly.

quote: The message that we can simply scale our way to AGI is incredibly attractive to investors because it puts money as the central (and sufficient) force needed to advance.

quote: Getting to AGI will likely take still more breakthroughs.
claim: Achieving AGI will likely require additional fundamental breakthroughs beyond current approaches.

quote: The code construction itself is still produced by a system that is highly dependent on similarity to training examples. In easy, familiar cases, the results can be astonishing. In problems that diverge from easy and familiar cases, the constructed code can be buggy, or altogether flawed.
claim: LLM-generated code is highly dependent on similarity to training examples and becomes buggy or flawed on problems that diverge from familiar cases.

quote: Leaving our lives in the hands of LLMs that lack explicit models is literally putting our lives at risk.

quote: Because Claude doesn't know how to relate words on a page [such as "biological weapons") with a model of human biology or a world model of its own conversional world (the world of discourse and user interaction) – it is at a loss to reliably enforce its own instructions.
claim: LLMs cannot reliably enforce their own safety instructions (system prompts) because they lack world models to connect words with their referents

quote: In some ways LLMs far exceed humans, but in other ways, they are still no match for an ant. Without robust cognitive models of the world, they should never be fully trusted.

quote: a deep understanding of cognitive science will be a key ingredient in the development of AGI

quote: my intuition is that such functions will not suffice for AGI. My use of the term world model is much closer to what you might find a physics engine, with representations of particular entities and their properties.

quote: It's great model, as far as LLMs go, topping most benchmarks, but it's certainly not AGI

trigger: Ilya Sutskever interview on scaling limits

trigger: Ilya Sutskever interview on scaling limits

trigger: Ilya Sutskever interview on scaling limits

claim: All major AI companies are repeating the same experiment of building ever-larger LLMs hoping to reach AGI, despite consistent failure to solve core issues.

trigger: Ilya Sutskever interview on scaling limits

trigger: Ilya Sutskever interview on scaling limits

trigger: Ilya Sutskever interview on scaling limits

claim: The machine learning community's arrogant exclusion of cognitive science and other fields has contributed to the current AI bubble and its potential economic fallout.

trigger: Ilya Sutskever interview on scaling limits
quote: we may have wasted a trillion dollars and several years to rediscover what cognitive science already knew
claim: The AI industry may have wasted a trillion dollars and several years rediscovering what cognitive science already knew about the limits of pattern-matching approaches.

trigger: Ilya Sutskever interview on scaling limits
quote: the impact of a deflated AI bubble could be immense
claim: The deflation of the AI bubble could have immense economic impact, potentially causing a recession.

quote: I think a fair case can be made that it is not what it has often been cracked up to be, and probably never will be.
claim: ChatGPT is not what it has been hyped to be and probably never will be.

quote: I just don't happen to think large language models like ChatGPT will get us there.
claim: Large language models like ChatGPT will not achieve artificial general intelligence.

quote: I think that the recurring core technical problems that we have seen (as discussed below) with LLMs aren't going way; instead they inherent to the technology.
claim: The recurring core technical problems of LLMs (hallucinations, unreliable reasoning, alignment issues) are inherent to the technology and will not go away.

quote: AGI (AI with the breadth and resourcesfulness of human intelligence) is not coming soon.
claim: AGI is not coming soon.

quote: productivity studies sometimes show a 30% gain, but none have come close to 10x (1000% gain)
claim: AI productivity gains for workers are around 30% at best, nowhere near the 10x gains that were promised.

quote: The results are disappointing because the underlying tech is unreliable, And that's been obvious from the start.
claim: The disappointing results of generative AI adoption stem from the underlying technology being unreliable.

quote: Three years and nearly a trillion dollars later almost of all it remains true.
claim: The core limitations Marcus identified in December 2022 (unreliability, hallucinations, poor reasoning, alignment problems) remain true three years and nearly a trillion dollars later.

quote: the same problems have continued to plague every single model since—from GPT 4.0 to GPT 4.1 to GPT 4.5 and GPT-5 (and many others) to all the variations on Claude, all the variations on Gemini, all the variations on Grok, all the variations on llama, all the variations on DeepSeek, and so on.
claim: The same core problems (hallucinations, unreliable reasoning, alignment issues) have plagued every LLM model from every company, including GPT variants, Claude, Gemini, Grok, Llama, and DeepSeek.

quote: Hundreds of models, always the same failure modes.
claim: Across hundreds of LLM models, the same failure modes persist.

quote: LLMs have undoubtedly gotten quantitively better, but qualitively the core problems remain.
claim: LLMs have improved quantitatively but qualitatively the core problems of hallucination, unreliable reasoning, and poor generalization remain.

quote: deep learning (the tech underling large language models) is a better ladder; but a better ladder doesn't necessarily get you to the moon.
claim: Deep learning / LLMs represent incremental improvement that cannot reach the goal of AGI, like a better ladder that cannot get you to the moon.

quote: My verdict, in short, is that ChatGPT is a trillion dollar experiment that has failed.
claim: ChatGPT is a trillion-dollar experiment that has failed to solve the core cognitive problems.

quote: ChatGPT has failed to make profits for companies like OpenAI.
claim: ChatGPT has failed to make profits for OpenAI due to the discrepancy between reliability and the immense costs of internet-scale data dependence.

quote: Ultimately though, what LLMs are great at seeming is plausible – while being much less good at getting their facts straight.
claim: LLMs excel at seeming plausible but are poor at factual accuracy.

quote: The gulf between how impressive something *seems* and how useful something *is* has never been greater.
claim: The gap between how impressive LLMs seem in demos and how useful they actually are is historically large.

quote: ChatGPT obviously lacks the reliability of calculators (which is something the field should aspire to)
claim: ChatGPT lacks the reliability of calculators, and the field should aspire to calculator-level reliability.

quote: any normal seven-year old develops a rich model of the world, including the objects they see, the people they interact with and so on, and even how their bodies work. GPT is still just faking it.
claim: Even a normal seven-year-old develops a richer model of the world than GPT, which is still faking understanding.

quote: ChatGPT has never grown up, in the sense of addressing the core challenges that I have set out. And on its own (without the aid of neurosymbolic systems and world models) probably never will.
claim: ChatGPT will probably never address its core challenges without the aid of neurosymbolic systems and world models.

quote: large language models have become a commodity; LLM profits outside of Nvidia are hard to find
claim: Large language models have become a commodity, and profits from LLMs outside of Nvidia are hard to find.

quote: the reality is OpenAI has ridden to a $500 billion valuation on hopes, not profits. And those hopes are slipping away, as usage declines and others catch up.
claim: OpenAI's $500 billion valuation is based on hopes rather than profits, and those hopes are slipping as usage declines and competitors catch up.

quote: In the end LLMs will continue to exist, no matter what; they will get cheaper. And they will find some utility (indeed they are already have). But they will never be AGI, and they never be much more than commodity.
claim: LLMs will continue to exist and get cheaper, finding some utility, but will never achieve AGI and will never be more than a commodity.

quote: The natural home for ChatGPT turns out to be… demos.
claim: ChatGPT's natural strength is in demos and first impressions rather than reliable production use.

quote: Amateurs can in fact now build prototypes, but the auto-generated code tends to be shaky and few amateurs can make those prototypes robust.
claim: LLMs enable amateurs to build prototypes, but the auto-generated code is shaky and few amateurs can make those prototypes robust.

quote: Anyone assuming that everything is simultaneously rising exponentially, and that it will continue to do so indefinitely, is a fool.
claim: The assumption that AI progress is rising exponentially on all fronts and will continue indefinitely is incorrect; different benchmarks show different patterns of progress.

quote: if Sustekver and I (and at this point many others) are right that scaling is largely played out, a decline in demand is sure to follow
claim: If scaling is largely played out, a decline in demand for AI compute is sure to follow.

quote: GenAI is like a rerun of that situation, an order or two of magnitude more hype, coupled with equal naïveté about what a cognitively adequate solution would actually look like, only this time with an extra decimal place, potentially a trillion dollars down the drain
claim: Generative AI repeats the pattern of driverless car hype but at one or two orders of magnitude greater scale, potentially wasting a trillion dollars.

quote: For the love of Darwin, please let's spend the next eight years considering new approaches, not tanking the economy on more of the same.
claim: The AI field should spend the next eight years pursuing new approaches rather than continuing to scale LLMs.

trigger: Owain Evans research on LLM vulnerabilities

trigger: Owain Evans research on LLM vulnerabilities
quote: There is no way in Darwin's green earth that we are ever going to be able to patch what is likely to be an endless list of vulnerabilities.

trigger: Owain Evans research on LLM vulnerabilities
quote: Putting society in the hands of giant, superficial correlation machines is not going to end well.

quote: One of the costliest mistakes in scientific history is coming to an end

quote: these systems would never be reliable enough, and that even with more data they would have trouble with hallucinations, factuality, reasoning, outliers, and generalization.

quote: coding tools sometimes impair productivity rather than increasing it

quote: We could easily wind up with too many data centers, and a lot of chips that rapidly lose their value.

quote: In the worst case, as I have warned before, we may hear shouts of 'too big to fail' and calls for government bailouts.

quote: And, honestly, I think that is the most likely scenario.

quote: Going all in on Generative AI, without diversifying intellectually and economically, is likely an immense mistake.

quote: If September 2025 was peak bubble, 2026 will likely the year which it all falls apart.

quote: the economics don't make sense, and never will, in large part because of the core technical problems that I have stressed repeatedly here and elsewhere since 2019.
claim: The economics of LLMs do not make sense and never will because of inherent core technical problems.

quote: Without world models. you cannot achieve reliability. And without reliability, profits are limited.

quote: The technical problems are not new. And a trillion dollars or so of investment hasn't remedied them.
claim: A trillion dollars of investment has not remedied the core technical problems of LLMs.

quote: they have at last become widely recognized, not just as the transitory 'bugs' the industry wanted you to think of, but as inherent limitations that flow from the very design of LLMs they really are.
claim: LLM problems like hallucinations and unreliable reasoning are now widely recognized as inherent design limitations rather than transitory bugs.

quote: Once the implications are fully appreciated, the whole thing will begin to unwind.
claim: Once the full implications of LLMs' inherent limitations are appreciated, the AI bubble will begin to unwind.
